confirmed.title: OP_SCOPE/OP_SELECT/OP_SPAWN sub-chunk index truncated to 8 bits with no overflow guard -> VM executes an arbitrary constant as a Chunk*
confirmed.trigger: A Lattice program whose enclosing chunk already holds >256 constants (e.g. ~300 distinct string literals, distinct big-int literals outside -128..127, or ~300 distinct referenced global names) followed by a concurrency form, e.g.:  `let a0=1000; let a1=1001; ... let a299=1299;  scope { spawn { print("x") } }`. The spawn/scope sub-chunk is added with chunk_add_constant (VAL_CLOSURE, never deduped), so its index is >=256 and `(uint8_t)` truncates it. There is no 16-bit form for these operands and no guard (unlike emit_constant_idx). chunk_verify is NOT run on freshly compiled chunks (only on deserialized .latc, src/latc.c:603).
confirmed.consequence: The VM (src/stackvm.c:7940 `Chunk *body = (Chunk*)frame->chunk->constants[sync_idx].as.closure.native_fn;`, also :7956/:7972 and the OP_SELECT handler :8039) reads the truncated index, which now points at an unrelated constant (string/int/array). It reinterprets that constant's union as a closure and executes `.as.closure.native_fn` as a Chunk* via stackvm_run. For a non-closure constant native_fn is garbage union bits -> wild/controlled pointer dereference and execution -> crash or potential control-flow hijack.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/critical] CONFIRMED reachable and exploitable from ordinary Lattice source (no malformed bytecode needed). Static: src/stackcompiler.c emits the OP_SCOPE/OP_SPAWN/OP_SELECT sub-chunk operands with raw `emit_byte((uint8_t)add_chunk_constant(...))` (lines 2100, 2103, 2134, 2150, 2156-2158, 2186-2189, 2194) — no overflow guard and, unlike emit_constant_idx (lines 104-114), no 16-bit form. add_chunk_constant (313-322) stores a VAL_CLOSURE via chunk_add_constant (chunk.c:109-142), which deduplicates only STR/INT/FLOAT, so closures always append and the returned index grows past 255. stack_compile (2990+) puts all top-level constants in one chunk, so a top-level concurrency form shares that pool. The VM (stackvm.c:7916/7940/7956/7972 OP_SCOPE, 8030-8040 OP_SELECT) reads the operand as a single READ_BYTE and does `(Chunk*)frame->chunk->constants[idx].as.closure.native_fn` then stackvm_run with no bounds/type check. chunk_verify runs only on deserialized .latc (latc.c:603), never on fresh compiles, and the truncation has already corrupted the byte at compile time anyway. Empirical: with the clean production clat, a plain .lat file of 250 top-level `let aN=\"str_N\"` followed by `scope { spawn { print(\"x\") } }` exits 139 (SIGSEGV); instrumentation showed the spawn closure was real constant index 500 but the emitted operand byte was 244 (500 & 0xFF), landing on a type-3 (non-closure, VAL_CLOSURE=6) constant whose union bits are then executed as a Chunk*. n=100 (index 200, no truncation) runs correctly. This is a genuine type-confusion / wild-pointer-as-code-structure with a deterministic crash and attacker-influenced target constant (integers/strings can be steered into the truncated slot), so critical stands. (Debug edit reverted; tree clean.) || [high/critical] Mechanism re-derived from the actual code and reproduced empirically.

CODE: At src/stackcompiler.c the OP_SPAWN (line 2100), OP_SCOPE (2134, 2150, 2156-2158) and OP_SELECT (2186, 2189, 2194) handlers all store sub-chunks via add_chunk_constant() (defined at 313-322), which builds a VAL_CLOSURE (body=NULL, native_fn=ch) and calls chunk_add_constant(). chunk_add_constant (src/chunk.c:109-142) only deduplicates VAL_STR/VAL_INT/VAL_FLOAT; VAL_CLOSURE falls through to `idx = c->const_len++`, so every sub-chunk gets a fresh, monotonically increasing index that can exceed 255. The returned size_t is cast `(uint8_t)` at every emit site with NO 16-bit form and NO overflow guard — unlike emit_constant_idx (104-114), which emits OP_*_16 for idx>255 and errors above 65535. So the operand silently truncates mod 256.

The VM (src/stackvm.c OP_SCOPE: sync_idx/spawn_idx READ_BYTE at 7915-7918, used at 7940/7956/7972; OP_SELECT chan/body/binding bytes at 8037-8040) indexes `frame->chunk->constants[idx].as.closure.native_fn` and casts to Chunk*, then stackvm_run() executes it. When the truncated index lands on a non-closure constant (VAL_INT/VAL_STR), `.as.closure.native_fn` reinterprets the union's int_val/str_val storage as a Chunk* — classic type confusion — and the VM dereferences/executes it.

REPRO: Generated `let a0=1000; ... let a299=1299; scope { spawn { print("x") } }`. The 300 distinct int literals are all >127 so each takes a constant slot (stackcompiler.c:465-468), plus 300 global-name strings, pushing the script chunk's const pool well past 256 before the spawn sub-chunk is added. ./clat -> SIGSEGV (exit 139). 3-constant control runs clean (prints x/done, exit 0). ./clat_asan -> "runtime error: member access within null pointer of type 'Chunk'" followed by "AddressSanitizer: SEGV on unknown address 0x0 ... in stackvm_run ... stackvm_spawn_thread_fn" — i.e. the type-confused Chunk* from the spawn-index path. chunk_verify is not run on freshly compiled chunks, so nothing catches the bad operand.

This is a genuine, source-triggerable type-confusion / wild-pointer execution in the default execution backend. Critical severity stands: a valid (if large) program causes the VM to reinterpret a constant's union bits (source-controlled string pointers / integers) as an executable Chunk*, yielding a reliable crash and a CFI violation with potential for worse. || [high/high] Verified against current source and reproduced empirically. add_chunk_constant (stackcompiler.c:313) stores a VAL_CLOSURE via chunk_add_constant (chunk.c:109), which only dedups VAL_STR/INT/FLOAT, so closure-constant indices grow unbounded. The OP_SCOPE/OP_SPAWN/OP_SELECT emit sites (stackcompiler.c:2100,2134,2150,2157-2158,2186,2189,2194) cast that index with a bare (uint8_t) and emit NO wide form and NO guard — unlike emit_constant_idx (:104-114) which has an OP_*_16 path. include/stackopcode.h:123-124 confirms every OP_SCOPE/OP_SELECT operand is exactly 1 byte. The VM (stackvm.c:7940/7956/7972 and the OP_SELECT handler :8036-8040) reads (Chunk*)constants[idx].as.closure.native_fn with no type-tag or bounds check. chunk_verify only runs on deserialized .latc (latc.c:603), never on freshly compiled chunks (and post-truncation the index is 0-255, often still in range, so verify wouldn't catch it anyway). Integer literals outside [-128,127] become deduped VAL_INT constants (:464-468), so ~300 big-int lets put the enclosing chunk past 256 constants. Built the ASan binary and ran `let a0=1000;...let a299=1299; scope{spawn{print("x")}}`: it crashes with UBSan 'member access within null pointer of type Chunk' then ASan SEGV at 0x0 inside stackvm_spawn_thread_fn->stackvm_run, while the identical concurrency form with few constants runs fine. The truncated spawn index pointed at a VAL_INT constant whose union bits were reinterpreted as a Chunk* and executed — the exact claimed type confusion. I could not refute it. Corrected severity to high rather than critical: it is a genuine memory-safety type-confusion (wild/null code-pointer deref and execution), but the demonstrated outcome is a crash and reliable control-flow hijack was not shown; it requires a program with >256 constants plus a concurrency form (achievable but not trivial). Fix: add an overflow guard/compile_error like emit_constant_idx, or introduce 16-bit operand forms for these opcodes.

confirmed.title: Field/method/struct/enum-name constant indices truncated to 8 bits → wild-pointer read in regvm GETFIELD/INVOKE
confirmed.code:
uint16_t field_ki = add_constant(value_string(e->as.field_access.field));
                emit_ABC(ROP_GETFIELD, dst, obj_reg, (uint8_t)field_ki, line);
confirmed.trigger: A Lattice program run with `clat --regvm` whose chunk accumulates >256 constants before a struct/map field access. Proven PoC: `struct S { field: int }` then 300 distinct big-int locals (`let a0 = 1000000` ... each >32767 so it becomes an int CONSTANT) then `let s = S{field:5}; print(s.field)`. The constant index of the field name "field" is ~602; `(uint8_t)field_ki` truncates it to 90, which addresses the integer constant 1000045. ASan: SEGV (READ) in strcmp at regvm.c:3614, x1=0xf426d (=1000045).
confirmed.consequence: add_constant returns a 16-bit index (REGVM_CONST_MAX=65536) but ROP_GETFIELD packs the field-name key into the 8-bit C operand (same for ROP_SETFIELD B, ROP_INVOKE/INVOKE_LOCAL/INVOKE_GLOBAL method name at lines 1474/1500/1537/2019/2038/2060, ROP_NEWSTRUCT/NEWENUM name keys, ROP_FREEZE_VAR/THAW_VAR/SUBLIMATE_VAR name keys). When the constant table exceeds 256 entries the high bits are silently dropped, so the regvm reads `frame->chunk->constants[c].as.str_val` (regvm.c:3609) for a different, non-string constant; an int/float constant's bytes are reinterpreted as a char* and strcmp dereferences it → deterministic wild-pointer read / crash (and arbitrary-address read primitive). No verification runs on in-memory regvm chunks, so the compiler output is fully trusted.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] VERIFIED AND REPRODUCED. The cited code is accurate: src/regcompiler.c:1024-1025 (and the parallel sites for SETFIELD/INVOKE/NEWSTRUCT/NEWENUM/FREEZE/THAW/SUBLIMATE) call add_constant() which returns a 16-bit index (guarded only at REGVM_CONST_MAX=65536 in regcompiler.c:228-235), then truncate it with (uint8_t)field_ki into the 8-bit C operand of emit_ABC (emit_ABC takes uint8_t a,b,c). regchunk_add_constant (regvm.c:126) deduplicates equal int/string/float constants but distinct large ints are not collapsed, so a chunk can accumulate >256 constants before a field access. The regvm GETFIELD handler (regvm.c:3605-3614) reads frame->chunk->constants[c].as.str_val and strcmp()s it, so a truncated C that lands on a non-string (int) constant reinterprets the int64 bytes as a char* and dereferences -> wild-pointer read.

REACHABILITY: I searched upstream for a guard and found the project DOES have a verifier, regchunk_verify (regvm.c:7540), whose RCK_STR(C) check for ROP_GETFIELD (regvm.c:7382) would reject a non-string field-key constant. BUT it is invoked in only one place: src/latc.c:1002, the .rlatc deserialization path ("Reject untrusted bytecode... before it can reach the register-VM"). The in-memory compile-and-run path (main.c:268 reg_compile -> main.c:290 regvm_run, no verify call) does NOT run it. So the compiler output is trusted directly, exactly as the finder states.

The 256-register cap (REGVM_REG_MAX=256, alloc_reg() errors at regcompiler.c:147) does NOT block reachability: the bug needs >256 CONSTANTS, not >256 live registers. I built a PoC using a single reassigned local (`let x=0; x=1000000; ... x=1000619; struct S{field:int}; let s=S{field:5}; print(s.field)`) — ~620 distinct int constants in 1-2 registers. Under `clat --regvm` it exits 139 (SIGSEGV); under ASan it crashes precisely as claimed: "SEGV READ on 0x00000000000f426d (=1000045) in strcmp <- regvm_dispatch <- regvm_run", with x1/x19=0xf426d, i.e. the int constant 1000045 dereferenced as a char*. The default bytecode VM also crashed on the same input (likely the same 8-bit-truncation pattern in src/compiler.c — a separate but related issue).

No upstream type check, bound, clone, or validator prevents the stated trigger on the regvm path. Corrected severity: high rather than critical — it is a genuine, deterministic memory-safety violation (wild-pointer read / arbitrary-address-read primitive whose address equals a program-controlled constant) reachable from an ordinary .lat source, but the demonstrated impact is a read-dereference crash/DoS (no arbitrary write; the dereferenced value is one the source already contains), and triggering it requires authoring the Lattice program being run. || [high/critical] Mechanism re-derived from source and reproduced under ASan. add_constant (regcompiler.c:228-236) returns a 16-bit index (limit REGVM_CONST_MAX=65536, regvm.h:16). At regcompiler.c:1024-1025 the field-name index is packed via (uint8_t)field_ki into the 8-bit C operand of REG_ENCODE_ABC (regopcode.h:18 gives C only bits 24-31), so any index >255 is silently truncated. The regvm GETFIELD handler (regvm.c:3605-3614) reads frame->chunk->constants[c].as.str_val and strcmp's it; the LatValue union (value.h:55-62) overlays int64_t int_val and char *str_val at offset 0, so a truncated index landing on an int constant reinterprets the integer as a char*. No verifier protects this path: regchunk_verify (with RCK_STR guards at regvm.c:7382 etc.) is called ONLY from regchunk_deserialize for .rlatc files (latc.c:1002); the in-memory --regvm path (main.c:286-290) calls regvm_run directly with no verification. Int literals in [-32768,32767] use ROP_LOADI immediates (regcompiler.c:556-561) while larger ints become constants, so 300 big-int locals push the field-name index past 255 exactly as claimed. I built the described PoC and ran it under clat_asan --regvm: AddressSanitizer reports SEGV READ in strcmp inside regvm_dispatch with x1=0x00000000000f426d (=1000045), matching the finder's prediction bit-for-bit. The same (uint8_t)(...&0xFF) truncation is present for INVOKE_GLOBAL/INVOKE_LOCAL/INVOKE name keys (lines 1474-1475,1500-1501,1537) and SETFIELD/NEWSTRUCT/NEWENUM. This is a genuine, deterministic type-confusion wild-pointer read and an attacker-controlled arbitrary-address read primitive; critical severity is correct. || [high/high] Confirmed against source. regcompiler.c:1024-1025 packs a 16-bit constant index into the 8-bit ROP_GETFIELD C operand via (uint8_t)field_ki. add_constant (regcompiler.c:228-236) permits indices up to REGVM_CONST_MAX-1 = 65535 (regvm.h:16), and emit_ABC's c operand is truly 8 bits (REG_ENCODE_ABC `<<24`, REG_GET_C `&0xFF`). regchunk_add_constant (regvm.c:126-155) dedups only equal values, so 300 distinct big-int literals (>32767 → add_constant(value_int) at regcompiler.c:559) each allocate a distinct constant, pushing the "field" string constant index past 255; e.g. 602 & 0xFF = 90. At runtime GETFIELD (regvm.c:3605-3614) does `field_name = constants[c].as.str_val` with the truncated c and strcmp(...) on it; since int_val and str_val share the union (value.h:55-60), an int constant 1000045 is read as pointer 0x000F426D → wild-pointer dereference / SEGV READ, matching the reported x1=0xf426d. The finder's pivotal claim that no verification runs on in-memory chunks is correct: the verifier reg_verify_operands/regchunk_verify (which would reject this via RCK_STR(C) at regvm.c:7382) is invoked ONLY in regchunk_deserialize for untrusted .rlatc files (latc.c:1002, comment: dispatch 'trusts every operand'); the --regvm in-memory path goes reg_compile→regvm_run directly (main.c:268-290) with no verify call. I found no clone/bound/mask/early-return that prevents this — the only guard (>=65536) is far too loose for an 8-bit field. The same (uint8_t) truncation recurs at SETFIELD/INVOKE/NEWSTRUCT/NEWENUM/FREEZE_VAR name keys (e.g. regcompiler.c:1156,1195). Genuine type-confusion/wild-pointer-read memory-safety bug. Down-rated critical→high only because it is confined to the explicitly non-default --regvm 'POC' backend (main.c:262); the default bytecode VM is unaffected.

confirmed.title: net_tcp_read_bytes: malloc(count+1) integer overflow -> heap buffer overflow with peer-controlled data
confirmed.trigger: Lattice program: `let fd = tcp_connect(host, port); tcp_write(fd, req); let s = tcp_read_bytes(fd, -1)`. The binding at src/eval.c:3839 / src/runtime.c:1097 converts the Int64 argument with `(size_t)args[1].as.int_val` and performs NO range check, so n = -1 becomes count = SIZE_MAX. `malloc(count + 1)` == `malloc(0)` returns a ~16-byte minimum chunk (non-NULL, so the !buf guard passes). The recv loop then `recv()`s up to `count - total` (SIZE_MAX) bytes; whatever the peer sends (e.g. 4 KB) is written starting at offset 0 of the 0-byte allocation. Equally reachable when a program forwards an attacker-supplied Content-Length header into tcp_read_bytes.
confirmed.consequence: Heap buffer overflow: peer/attacker fully controls both the overflow length (bytes available on the socket) and the overflow contents (the bytes sent). The trailing `buf[total] = '\0'` also writes out of bounds. Leads to heap corruption and likely arbitrary code execution / crash.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] Verified against the actual source. src/net.c:215-236 is exactly as claimed: net_tcp_read_bytes does `char *buf = malloc(count + 1)` with count a size_t, guarded only by is_tracked(fd) — no bound on count. The dispatch in ALL paths performs a type-only check and an unchecked cast: src/eval.c:3833-3839 (`args[1].type != VAL_INT` then `(size_t)args[1].as.int_val`) and src/runtime.c:1094-1097 (same, registered for the default stackvm and regvm at runtime.c:4318). int_val is int64_t (include/value.h:56), so passing -1 is a valid VAL_INT and (size_t)(-1) == SIZE_MAX. malloc(SIZE_MAX+1) == malloc(0) returns a non-NULL minimum chunk on glibc/macOS, so the `!buf` guard passes; the recv loop then calls recv(fd, buf, SIZE_MAX, 0) — the kernel caps the length internally (Linux to ~0x7ffff000, macOS bounds by available data) and copies the peer's available bytes (socket-buffer-sized, tens to hundreds of KB) into the ~0-24 byte allocation, a peer-controlled heap buffer overflow, plus the trailing buf[total]='\0' OOB write. I searched for any clamp/range validation on count in net.c and both dispatchers and found none — no upstream guard exists. The path is genuinely reachable from a real Lattice program (`tcp_read_bytes(fd, -1)`, or any computation yielding a -1/underflowed count). Downgraded critical->high because the malloc wrap requires count == SIZE_MAX *exactly* (i.e. -1); any other large value either allocates successfully (safe) or fails malloc and hits the !buf guard, so the 'attacker-supplied Content-Length' scenario only fires if the parsed length is precisely -1. It remains a real, reachable, peer-controllable heap overflow caused by a missing bounds check on a length argument flowing into malloc+recv. || [high/critical] Mechanism re-derived from source and confirmed. src/net.c:215-236 (native, non-Emscripten build) has NO upper-bound check on `count`: it does `malloc(count + 1)` then loops `while (total < count) recv(fd, buf+total, count-total, 0)`. The conversion path has no range check at either backend: src/eval.c:3839 (tree-walk) and src/runtime.c:1097 (bytecode VM native, same VMNativeFn signature) both pass `(size_t)args[1].as.int_val`, and `int_val` is `int64_t` (include/value.h:56). Passing -1 yields `(size_t)-1 == SIZE_MAX`. Then `count + 1 == 0`, so `malloc(0)` returns a non-NULL ~16-byte chunk on glibc/macOS/Windows and the `if(!buf)` guard at net.c:222 passes. The loop `while (total < SIZE_MAX)` then recv()s up to SIZE_MAX bytes into that 0-byte allocation, writing peer-controlled bytes from offset 0 (heap overflow, attacker controls both length and contents), and `buf[total]='\0'` (net.c:234) is an additional OOB write. The only nuance: the small-allocation wraparound occurs precisely at count==SIZE_MAX (int_val==-1); other huge values keep count+1 huge so malloc fails and is caught — but -1 (the finder's primary trigger) is exactly the wrapping value, and is reachable directly via `tcp_read_bytes(fd, -1)` or by forwarding a peer-supplied 64-bit length field (0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF) / parsed negative Content-Length. The second net_tcp_read_bytes at net.c:361 is just the __EMSCRIPTEN__ stub and is irrelevant. No clone/borrow, bounds, type, or control-flow misread by the finder. Genuine integer-overflow-to-heap-overflow with attacker-controlled data; critical is appropriate. || [high/high] Verified against the actual file. src/net.c:215-236 (the active POSIX impl; the duplicate at net.c:361 is the __EMSCRIPTEN__ stub returning an error) does `char *buf = malloc(count + 1)` with no upper-bound check, then `while (total < count) recv(fd, buf+total, count-total, 0)`. Both runtime bindings pass the size unchecked: eval.c:3839 and runtime.c:1097 both do `(size_t)args[1].as.int_val`, and the only argument validation (`args[1].type != VAL_INT` at eval.c:3833 / runtime.c:1095) rejects non-integers but NOT negative/out-of-range integers. I searched the whole path for a clamp/guard and found none.

Mechanism confirmed: passing n = -1 yields count = (size_t)(-1) = SIZE_MAX. `count + 1` wraps (unsigned) to 0, so `malloc(0)` is called. On the target platforms (darwin per env, and glibc) malloc(0) returns a non-NULL minimal chunk, so the `!buf` guard passes. The loop then recv()s up to `count - total` (~SIZE_MAX) bytes into a 0/16-byte allocation; the peer fully controls both the length (bytes available on the socket) and the contents (bytes sent), producing a heap buffer overflow, and the trailing `buf[total] = '\0'` is also OOB. This is genuinely triggerable and not refutable.

Notes that slightly temper (but do not negate) the finding: the overflow specifically requires count == SIZE_MAX so that count+1 wraps to a small allocation that malloc actually returns. For other large counts (e.g. an attacker-supplied multi-GB Content-Length), malloc(count+1) is a huge request that fails and hits the `out of memory` guard (graceful error, no overflow) — so the 'forward an attacker Content-Length' framing only triggers the overflow if that value is exactly -1/SIZE_MAX. However, n = -1 is a very natural 'read all remaining' idiom (cf. Python read(-1)), so a non-malicious program can hit this, and the contents are fully peer-controlled — a real memory-safety hole in the runtime. I downgrade critical->high only because triggering requires the program to pass n = -1/SIZE_MAX rather than being a pure remote-without-program-cooperation primitive; critical is defensible given full attacker control of overflow contents.

confirmed.title: net_tls_read_bytes: malloc(count+1) integer overflow -> heap buffer overflow (OpenSSL and Schannel)
confirmed.trigger: Lattice program over TLS: `let fd = tls_connect(host, 443); tls_write(fd, req); let s = tls_read_bytes(fd, -1)`. Binding at src/eval.c:3966 / src/runtime.c:1177 passes `(size_t)args[1].as.int_val` unchecked; n = -1 -> count = SIZE_MAX -> malloc(0). SSL_read then writes up to `(int)(count - total)` server-controlled plaintext bytes into the 0-byte allocation. The Schannel build has the identical defect at src/tls.c:538 (`char *result = malloc(count + 1);`).
confirmed.consequence: Heap buffer overflow with TLS-peer-controlled contents; `buf[total]` also OOB. Heap corruption / RCE / crash. Especially dangerous because programs commonly compute the byte count from a server-supplied Content-Length, making the overflow remotely triggerable by a malicious HTTPS endpoint.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/critical] Reachability is confirmed: int_val is int64_t (include/value.h:56) and both bindings (src/eval.c:3960-3966, src/runtime.c:1172-1177) validate only VAL_INT then pass `(size_t)args[1].as.int_val` with NO sign/range check. A program calling `tls_read_bytes(fd, -1)` yields count = (size_t)(-1) = SIZE_MAX, so `malloc(count+1) = malloc(0)`. (Precise trigger is arg == -1; other negatives produce malloc(SIZE_MAX-k) which fails -> NULL -> safe OOM; huge positives also OOM -> safe.)\n\nSchannel path (src/tls.c:537-578) is an unambiguous critical heap overflow: it does not pass count as a read length, it pulls a fresh server plaintext chunk via net_tls_read() (up to SCHAN_READ_BUF=65536, tls.c:423). The bounding clamp `take = (total+clen > count) ? count-total : clen` is defeated when count==SIZE_MAX (total+clen can't exceed SIZE_MAX), so take=clen and `memcpy(result+total, chunk, clen)` copies up to 64KB of TLS-peer-controlled plaintext into the 0-byte allocation, looping and writing further OOB until EOF/crash. Real attacker-controlled heap corruption on the Windows build.\n\nOpenSSL path (src/tls.c:127-148) is largely mitigated on supported versions: with count==SIZE_MAX the read length `(int)(count-total)` is -1, and modern OpenSSL SSL_read has `if (num < 0) return -1;` (since 1.1.0), so the loop breaks and only `buf[0]='\\0'` writes 1 byte into malloc(0) (benign on glibc/macOS min-chunk, ASan-detectable; could be worse on EOL 1.0.x). So the OpenSSL data-write mechanism as described is mostly defanged, but the Schannel overflow makes the overall finding genuinely real and critical. The 'remotely triggerable via Content-Length' framing is slightly overstated (server must induce the program to pass exactly -1, e.g. a parse sentinel or negative computed length), but the defect and reachability are real. || [high/high] ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED: `as.int_val` is int64_t (include/value.h:56). Both bindings — eval.c:3966 and runtime.c:1177 — pass `(size_t)args[1].as.int_val` to net_tls_read_bytes with NO sign/bounds check (only a VAL_INT type check). A Lattice program passing -1 therefore yields count = SIZE_MAX, and `malloc(count + 1)` (tls.c:133 OpenSSL, tls.c:538 Schannel) computes malloc(0). The integer overflow / under-allocation is real.

BUT the finder's exploitation mechanism for the PRIMARY cited location (OpenSSL, tls.c:127-148) is OVERSTATED. With count=SIZE_MAX the loop calls `SSL_read(buf, (int)(count - total))` = `SSL_read(buf, (int)SIZE_MAX)`; the `int` cast truncates SIZE_MAX to -1, not a large positive number. Modern OpenSSL (1.1.0+/3.x) `SSL_read` begins with `if (num < 0) { ... return -1; }`, so n <= 0 and the loop breaks immediately — NO server-controlled bytes are written. The finder's claim that "SSL_read then writes up to (int)(count-total) server-controlled plaintext bytes into the 0-byte allocation" does not hold on modern OpenSSL. What actually remains on the OpenSSL path is the single line `buf[total] = '\0'` with total=0, i.e. a 1-byte OOB null write into the malloc(0) region (ASan would flag it; benign on glibc/macOS due to 16-byte minimum chunk). The dramatic RCE/flood narrative is inaccurate for the build used on Linux/macOS/WASM. (On EOL OpenSSL 1.0.x, which lacks the num<0 guard, it could be worse, but that is version-dependent.)

The Schannel/Windows variant (tls.c:537-573), however, DOES exhibit exactly the described overflow: malloc(0), then `take = (total + clen > count) ? count - total : clen` evaluates `clen > SIZE_MAX` = false, so take = clen, and `memcpy(result + total, chunk, take)` copies server-controlled plaintext (clen up to ~8192 per net_tls_read, looping) into the 0-byte allocation — a genuine server-controlled heap buffer overflow. Since the project ships Windows builds, this is a real critical-class instance.

VERDICT: real=true (the missing count guard is a genuine memory-safety bug; an OOB write occurs on both builds, and the Schannel build is a true attacker-controlled heap overflow). Severity downgraded critical->high: the headline OpenSSL mechanism the finding centers on is inaccurate (int-truncation to -1 + SSL_read negative-length rejection reduce it to a 1-byte null OOB); the critical server-controlled overflow is confined to the Schannel/Windows path, and triggering requires the program to forward a negative/SIZE_MAX sentinel as count. Fix: reject count < 0 (and absurdly large counts) in the bindings or in net_tls_read_bytes. || [high/high] Confirmed root cause: bindings at src/eval.c:3960-3966 and src/runtime.c:1172-1177 only type-check VAL_INT and pass (size_t)args[1].as.int_val unchecked. A Lattice -1 becomes SIZE_MAX, so malloc(count+1) = malloc(SIZE_MAX+1) = malloc(0), which returns non-NULL and bypasses the `if(!buf)` guard. (Only count==SIZE_MAX wraps to a tiny alloc; any other huge count makes malloc fail and hit the NULL guard.)

I tried to refute and SUCCEEDED only for the OpenSSL path (src/tls.c:127-148, the Linux/macOS default build, #if LATTICE_HAS_TLS && !LATTICE_TLS_SCHANNEL): with count=SIZE_MAX the SSL_read length is (int)(count-total) = (int)SIZE_MAX = -1, and modern OpenSSL (1.1.0+, 2016) rejects num<0 with BAD_LENGTH returning -1, so n<=0 breaks the loop and zero server bytes are written. The only residual write is buf[0]='\0' into a malloc(0) region — benign on glibc/macOS (nonzero usable size), at most an ASan-flaggable 1-byte nit. The finder's stated OpenSSL mechanism (SSL_read writing (int)(count-total) server bytes) is factually wrong: that value is -1, not a large positive.

But the Schannel path (src/tls.c:537-578, Windows make WINDOWS=1, #elif LATTICE_TLS_SCHANNEL) is a GENUINE, exploitable heap overflow and cannot be refuted: result=malloc(count+1)=malloc(0); the loop `while(total<count)` calls net_tls_read (returns up to 64KB of server-DECRYPTED plaintext from DecryptMessage SECBUFFER_DATA, lines 505-507), computes take = (total+clen>count)? count-total : clen — and since 0+clen does NOT exceed SIZE_MAX, take=clen (unbounded) — then memcpy(result+total, chunk, take) copies attacker-controlled plaintext into the 0-byte heap buffer. No int-cast saves it here; net_tls_read isn't even passed the remaining-count limit. That is a remotely-triggerable, server-controlled heap buffer overflow on a shipped build.

Verdict real=true. Downgraded critical->high: the finder's blanket 'OpenSSL and Schannel, RCE' overstates impact — the default/most-deployed OpenSSL build is effectively mitigated to a benign 1-byte write by the SSL_read(num<0) guard. The dangerous server-controlled overflow is confined to the Windows/Schannel build and additionally requires the application to pass a negative/SIZE_MAX count to tls_read_bytes. Fix: range-check count (reject negative / cap to a sane max) in the binding or in net_tls_read_bytes before malloc(count+1), in both src/tls.c:133 and src/tls.c:538.

confirmed.title: STMT_ASSIGN: RHS value left un-rooted across resolve_lvalue's index evaluation can be GC-swept before being stored
confirmed.trigger:
Run under the tree-walker with GC stress: `clat --tree-walk --gc-stress prog.lat` where prog.lat is:
  fn idx() { let z = 0; return z }
  flux arr = [9, 9, 9]
  arr[idx()] = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
  print(arr[0])
The RHS array [1,2,3,4,5] is allocated in the fluid heap and held in `valr.value` (a C local, never GC_PUSH'd). resolve_lvalue() takes the EXPR_INDEX branch and calls eval_expr() on the index `idx()` (eval.c:645), which runs the function body through eval_block_stmts -> gc_maybe_collect -> gc_cycle. Since valr.value is in no GC root set, fluid_sweep free()s its `elems` buffer. `*target = valr.value` then stores a dangling array.
confirmed.consequence: Use-after-free: `print(arr[0])` reads the freed elems buffer; at scope teardown value_free(arr) frees the already-freed buffer again (double-free). Heap corruption / crash; reachable with a small program under --tree-walk --gc-stress (or natural heap pressure, since gc_maybe_collect also fires once total_bytes >= gc_threshold). Any `container[call_expr()] = heap_value` form is affected.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] CONFIRMED reachable and exploitable. Read the live code: STMT_ASSIGN at src/eval.c:10603 does `EvalResult valr = eval_expr(...value)` and never GC_PUSHes valr.value. For target `arr[idx()]` the EXPR_INDEX target is not a buffer and its index is not EXPR_RANGE, so both fast-path branches (10633, 10676) are skipped and control reaches the generic `resolve_lvalue(ev, target,...)` at 10804. resolve_lvalue's EXPR_INDEX branch evaluates the index FIRST (src/eval.c:640-645, with a comment explicitly noting eval_expr there 'may trigger GC'), so `idx()` runs via call_fn -> eval_block_stmts, which calls gc_maybe_collect at every statement (11297). Under --gc-stress (ev->gc_stress, 446) this forces gc_cycle, whose roots are only env + gc_roots shadow stack + saved_envs (389-403). valr.value is a bare C local in none of those; its elems buffer was lat_alloc/fluid_alloc-tracked with region_id==REGION_NONE (value.c:206-213, REGION_NONE==(size_t)-1 per value.h:51), so it is unmarked and swept. Then 10894-10895 `value_free(target); *target = valr.value` stores a dangling array; print(arr[0]) reads freed memory and teardown double-frees. I searched upstream for any guard (clone of the RHS, a GC_PUSH, a type/phase check that diverts the path, an index restriction) and found none on this path. I also empirically reproduced it: clat_asan --tree-walk --gc-stress on the exact trigger program reports heap-use-after-free with the freed-by stack fluid_sweep<-gc_maybe_collect<-eval_block_stmts<-call_fn<-...<-resolve_lvalue<-eval_stmt, allocated by value_array, UAF read in value_clone_impl via env_get during print(arr[0]) — matching the claim exactly. The working-tree src/eval.c diff is unrelated doc-comment text, so the live path is unchanged. Severity high rather than critical only because the affected mode (--tree-walk) is non-default (bytecode VM is default) and reliable triggering uses --gc-stress, though natural heap pressure (total_bytes>=gc_threshold) makes it reachable without the flag; the general form container[call_expr()] = heap_value is broadly affected. || [high/high] Mechanism re-derived from source AND empirically reproduced under ASan. In STMT_ASSIGN (src/eval.c:10602-10903), the RHS is captured at line 10603 into the C local `valr.value` and is never GC_PUSH'd anywhere in the block. gc_cycle (eval.c:382) only roots: env values, the gc_roots shadow stack (GC_PUSH), and saved caller envs; valr.value is in none. resolve_lvalue's EXPR_INDEX branch (eval.c:640-711) evaluates the index expression at line 645 BEFORE resolving the parent; for `arr[idx()] = [1,2,3,4,5]` this runs idx()'s body via eval_block_stmts, which calls gc_maybe_collect every statement (eval.c:11297). Under --gc-stress (ev->gc_stress, eval.c:446) this forces gc_cycle. The RHS array is allocated by value_array (value.c:206) via lat_alloc->fluid_alloc and tagged region_id=REGION_NONE ((size_t)-1), so gc_mark_value (eval.c:235) does NOT skip it and fluid_sweep (memory.c:114) free()s it as unmarked. Then *target = valr.value at line 10895 stores the dangling array (freed .elems). The buffer/slice pre-checks (10635/10679) only resolve .index.object (the ident `arr`), so they do not evaluate idx(); the GC-triggering eval is the main resolve_lvalue at line 10804. ASan reproduction with the exact trigger program reported heap-use-after-free: freed-by stack = fluid_sweep <- gc_maybe_collect(eval.c:448) <- eval_block_stmts(eval.c:11297) <- call_fn(eval.c:1143) <- resolve_lvalue(eval.c:645) <- eval_stmt(eval.c:10804); alloc stack = value_array <- EXPR_ARRAY(eval.c:8511) <- eval_stmt(eval.c:10603); UAF read during print(arr[0]) via env_get/value_clone. The finder misread nothing — ownership (no clone, borrowed C local), control flow (index eval before store), and the union member (VAL_ARRAY.elems) are all correct. Severity high not critical: confined to the non-default --tree-walk backend; deterministic trigger uses --gc-stress (natural heap pressure via gc_threshold can also fire it). || [high/high] CONFIRMED via ASan reproduction of the exact trigger (clat_asan --tree-walk --gc-stress on the 4-line program). I tried hard to refute and could not.

Source verification of the mechanism in src/eval.c STMT_ASSIGN (case at 10602):
- Line 10603 evaluates the RHS [1,2,3,4,5]. Its elems buffer is allocated in value_array (src/value.c:209) via lat_alloc -> fluid_alloc (src/memory.c:41), so it is a tracked fluid allocation. valr.value is a C local that is never GC_PUSH'd anywhere between 10603 and the resolve_lvalue at 10804 (the EXPR_IDENT, buffer, and slice fast-paths at 10607/10633/10676 do not apply to arr[idx()], and the buffer/slice checks only resolve the object `arr`, an EXPR_IDENT, which does not eval idx()).
- At 10804 resolve_lvalue is called on the full target arr[idx()]. resolve_lvalue's EXPR_INDEX branch (614-) evaluates the index expression first at line 645 (the comment even says it does so to avoid stale pointers from GC). idx()'s body runs through call_fn -> eval_block_stmts (11291), which calls gc_maybe_collect (11297). Under --gc-stress (gc_maybe_collect at 445 forces gc_cycle every time), gc_cycle (382) marks only roots from env, the gc_roots shadow stack, and saved_envs, then fluid_sweep (memory.c:114) free()s every unmarked allocation. The fluid heap has no nursery/pinning, so the freshly-allocated, unrooted RHS elems buffer is freed.
- Line 10894-10895: value_free(target); *target = valr.value stores the now-dangling array into arr[0]. print(arr[0]) then reads it.

ASan output matches exactly: allocated in value_array<-fluid_alloc; freed in fluid_sweep<-gc_maybe_collect<-eval_block_stmts<-call_fn<-eval_expr_inner(idx)<-resolve_lvalue<-eval_stmt; heap-use-after-free READ in value_clone_impl<-env_get<-eval_expr_inner for print(arr[0]). The secondary double-free at scope teardown was not reached only because ASan aborts on the first UAF, but the UAF is proven.

Severity high (not critical): it is a genuine heap UAF/double-free reachable from a tiny program, but only in the non-default tree-walker backend (bytecode VM is the default) and needs GC pressure (guaranteed under --gc-stress; otherwise requires total_bytes >= 1MB gc_threshold). Any `container[call_expr()] = heap_value` form under --tree-walk is affected.

confirmed.title: EXPR_CALL builtins: argument array popped off GC shadow stack before higher-order builtins (pipe/grow/assert_throws) run user closures
confirmed.trigger:
Run under the tree-walker with GC stress: `clat --tree-walk --gc-stress prog.lat` where prog.lat is:
  pipe(5, |x| { x * 2 }, |x| { x + 1 })
After GC_POP_N(ev, argc) at line 2081 the evaluated args (including both closure values, whose param_names array and name strings are fluid-heap allocated by value_closure) are no longer GC roots. pipe (eval.c:7287) calls call_closure on the first closure; its body `{ x * 2 }` goes through eval_block_stmts -> gc_maybe_collect -> gc_cycle, which sweeps the still-needed second closure (args[2]) param_names array/strings. The next loop iteration's call_closure(args[2], ...) then reads args[2].as.closure.param_names in env_define -> use-after-free.
confirmed.consequence: Use-after-free read of freed closure metadata (param_names array and parameter name strings), and on cleanup value_free(&args[i]) of a swept VAL_CLOSURE double-frees. Same root cause (un-rooted `args` across user-code eval) affects other higher-order builtins reachable after line 2081: `grow()` holds both `args` and its env_get clone `val` across call_closure (eval.c:2510), `assert_throws()` (7152), and the iterator `map`/`filter` builtins (5063/5081). Heap corruption / crash under --tree-walk --gc-stress or heap pressure.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] CONFIRMED real and reachable, verified both statically and dynamically (ASan repro).

Static path (src/eval.c): In the EXPR_CALL handler, args are evaluated and GC_PUSH'd (lines 2070-2080), then GC_POP_N(ev, argc) at line 2081 un-roots the entire args array before any builtin dispatch. The `pipe` builtin (lines 7274-7299) then iterates args[1..] calling call_closure(args[i].as.closure.param_names, ...) and does NOT re-push args onto the GC shadow stack. I verified there is no re-rooting between line 2081 and the pipe dispatch (the GC_PUSH at 7545/7609 belong to other builtins, not pipe). Closure metadata is genuinely fluid-heap allocated: value_closure (src/value.c:247-248) uses lat_alloc/lat_strdup, which route to fluid_alloc(g_heap->fluid) when a heap is active. call_closure (src/eval.c:1296-1358) only roots the caller env (saved_envs) and swaps ev->env to the closure's own captured_env; the *other* uncalled closures in `args` are reachable from nothing the GC marks (gc_cycle marks only env, gc_roots shadow stack, and saved_envs — lines 382-403). The first closure's body `{x*2}` runs through eval_block_stmts, which calls gc_maybe_collect at line 11297 (forced every statement under --gc-stress, main.c flag confirmed). fluid_sweep then frees args[2]'s param_names array/strings; the next loop iteration reads them -> UAF, and cleanup value_free(&args[i]) double-frees.

Dynamic confirmation: Ran the exact trigger `pipe(5, |x|{x*2}, |x|{x+1})` under `./clat_asan --tree-walk --gc-stress`. AddressSanitizer reports heap-use-after-free READ of size 8 in call_closure, freed by fluid_sweep<-gc_maybe_collect<-eval_block_stmts (first closure body) <-call_closure<-eval_expr_inner (pipe dispatch), previously allocated by value_closure<-fluid_alloc. The backtrace is an exact match to the claimed mechanism. Process exits 141 (crash).

No upstream guard prevents this: pipe/grow/assert_throws are plain builtins reachable from ordinary user source with no type/arity validation that would block the closure args, and EXPR_CLOSURE temporaries are not stored in any env. --gc-stress only makes the collection deterministic; the same UAF can fire under natural heap pressure once fluid->total_bytes crosses gc_threshold. Severity high is appropriate (memory-safety UAF + double-free, triggerable by trivial user code). || [high/high] CONFIRMED via ASan reproduction with the exact documented trigger (`clat --tree-walk --gc-stress` on `pipe(5, |x| { x * 2 }, |x| { x + 1 })`). Every link of the claimed mechanism holds against the actual code:

1) src/eval.c:2070-2081: args are evaluated with a per-iteration GC_PUSH(&args[i]) (line 2079) but then GC_POP_N(ev, argc) at line 2081 un-roots ALL of them before builtin dispatch. The push loop only protects earlier args during later-arg evaluation; after line 2081 nothing roots the args.

2) value_closure (src/value.c:244-255) allocates param_names via lat_alloc and each param name via lat_strdup. lat_alloc/lat_strdup (src/value.c:113-117, 140-146) route to fluid_alloc(g_heap->fluid, ...) during normal tree-walk execution, so closure param_names array + strings live in the fluid heap and are swept by gc_cycle/fluid_sweep.

3) pipe (src/eval.c:7283-7299) holds the raw args array across a loop of call_closure calls and does NOT re-root them. call_closure (src/eval.c:1272-1366) binds params (reads params[i] at line 1338) then evaluates the body via eval_expr (1354) -> EXPR_BLOCK -> eval_block_stmts, which calls gc_maybe_collect at line 11297. Under --gc-stress this always runs gc_cycle (eval.c:445-449).

4) gc_cycle (src/eval.c:382-439) marks roots only from ev->env, the shadow stack (ev->gc_roots), and saved_envs. The unbound second closure value (args[2]) is in none of these, so its fluid param_names allocation is unmarked and fluid_sweep frees it during the FIRST closure's body eval. The next loop iteration's call_closure(args[2].as.closure.param_names, ...) then reads freed memory at env_define (line 1338) -> use-after-free; the subsequent value_free(&args[2]) on the swept closure additionally double-frees.

ASan output exactly matches: heap-use-after-free READ at call_closure eval.c:1338, freed by fluid_sweep (memory.c:124) via gc_maybe_collect (eval.c:448) <- eval_block_stmts (eval.c:11297) <- call_closure (eval.c:1354) <- pipe (eval.c:7287), originally allocated by value_closure (value.c:247) at the arg-eval site (eval.c:2071). No clone/guard refutes it. The same root cause (args un-rooted after line 2081) applies to other higher-order builtins that run user closures (grow, assert_throws, map/filter). Severity confirmed high: genuine memory-safety UAF + double-free with a deterministic repro; can also fire under real heap pressure (gc_threshold), though it is gated behind the non-default --tree-walk backend, which is the only reason it is not critical. || [high/high] Confirmed REAL and reproduced with AddressSanitizer. I built a fresh ASan clat from current src/eval.c and ran the exact trigger `pipe(5, |x| { x * 2 }, |x| { x + 1 })` under `--tree-walk --gc-stress`, producing a definitive heap-use-after-free whose stack trace matches the finding exactly.

Verified the mechanism in code: (1) src/eval.c:2081 `GC_POP_N(ev, argc)` pops ALL evaluated args off the GC shadow stack immediately after the args-eval loop, before builtin dispatch. (2) value_closure (src/value.c:247-248) allocates the closure's param_names array and parameter name strings via lat_alloc/lat_strdup, which route to fluid_alloc (src/value.c:113-116) when g_heap is set — so closure metadata IS fluid-heap tracked and sweepable, refuting the 'borrowed AST memory' defense. (3) The closure value carries region_id==REGION_NONE, so gc_mark_value (src/eval.c:235) does not early-return and marks it normally; an unrooted closure is therefore genuinely collected. (4) In the pipe builtin (src/eval.c:7287), call_closure runs the FIRST closure's body `{ x*2 }`, which reaches eval_block_stmts -> gc_maybe_collect -> gc_cycle -> fluid_sweep; the SECOND closure (args[2]) is reachable from no root (not in ev->env, not on the shadow stack, not in saved_envs since anonymous args are never bound to a name), so fluid_sweep (src/memory.c:124) frees its param_names. (5) The next loop iteration's call_closure(args[2]...) reads the freed param_names[0] at env_define (src/eval.c:1338) -> UAF.

ASan output: freed by fluid_sweep memory.c:124 <- gc_maybe_collect eval.c:448 <- eval_block_stmts eval.c:11297; allocated by value_closure value.c:247 <- eval_expr_inner eval.c:9392/2071; UAF READ at call_closure eval.c:1338. Every refutation avenue (clone, region-id skip, re-root, gc_inhibit, AST-owned param names) was checked and failed. Severity high is appropriate: genuine memory-safety UAF (plus double-free on cleanup) affecting multiple higher-order builtins (pipe, grow, assert_throws, map/filter), reproducible under --tree-walk --gc-stress and reachable under ordinary heap pressure once total_bytes>=gc_threshold during the callbacks. Note it is tree-walker-only (default backend is the bytecode VM), which slightly narrows real-world exposure but does not make it non-exploitable.

confirmed.title: Off-by-one OOB read in match array-pattern with element(s) after a ...rest pattern
confirmed.consequence: For pattern elements positioned after a ...rest, the array index is computed one too high (correct value is arr_len - (pat_count - k)). The final post-rest element always reads scr.value.as.array.elems[arr_len], a heap out-of-bounds LatValue. That garbage value's `type` tag and `as.*` union (read at line 9742, then deep-cloned at 9752 for PAT_BINDING or compared at 9764 for PAT_LITERAL) are passed to value_clone_impl/value_equal, which dereference union pointer members (str_val, array.elems, strct.field_*, map.map, etc.). Result: out-of-bounds heap read leading to a crash, wild pointer dereference, or type confusion driven entirely by attacker-controlled Lattice source. Even when it does not crash, post-rest elements bind to the wrong (neighboring) array element, so [a, ...mid, b] silently produces incorrect bindings.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] VERIFIED REAL and reachable. The cited code at src/eval.c:9740-9742 reads:
  if (rest_idx >= 0 && (int)k > rest_idx) arr_idx = arr_len - (pat_count - 1 - k); else arr_idx = k;
  LatValue *elem = &scr.value.as.array.elems[arr_idx];
For post-rest pattern elements the correct mapping is arr_idx = arr_len - (pat_count - k); the code uses arr_len - (pat_count - 1 - k), which is exactly one too high. For the trailing element k = pat_count-1 this yields arr_idx = arr_len, an out-of-bounds LatValue.

No upstream guard prevents the trigger:
1. Parser (src/parser.c:1124-1144) parses array patterns by simply checking for TOK_DOTDOTDOT per element and pushing each ArrayPatElem; it imposes NO restriction that the rest element be last. So `[first, ...mid, last]` parses into elems[first(fixed), mid(rest), last(fixed)] with rest_idx=1, fixed_count=2.
2. The PAT_ARRAY length precondition at eval.c:9697-9709 only requires arr_len >= fixed_count when a rest is present (4 >= 2 here), so it does NOT block the buggy branch; execution falls straight into the off-by-one index at line 9740.
3. Tree-walk is a first-class supported mode: main.c:1505 sets tree_walk_mode on `--tree-walk`, main.c:238 routes to evaluator_run (this code), and it is part of the test matrix (`make test-tree-walk`). The default is the bytecode VM, so this only affects `--tree-walk` runs — that is the sole mitigant and the reason it isn't the default-path; but the mode is real, supported, and tested.

Empirically confirmed with the in-repo clat_asan binary on the exact trigger program:
classify([10,20,30,40]) with pattern [first, ...mid, last] under `--tree-walk` produces:
  AddressSanitizer: heap-buffer-overflow READ of size 4 at 4 bytes after the 288-byte array region (i.e. elems[4] of a 4-element array, 72 bytes/LatValue), inside value_clone_impl, called from eval_expr_inner's PAT_ARRAY handler (the value_deep_clone(elem) at eval.c:9752 for the PAT_BINDING `last`). Stack and offset match the claim exactly.

The OOB-read value's type tag and union are then passed into value_clone_impl (or value_equal for a PAT_LITERAL post-rest element), which dereferences union pointer members of garbage heap memory — a genuine memory-safety hazard (crash / type confusion), not merely an incorrect binding. Severity high is appropriate: attacker-controlled Lattice source triggers an OOB heap read with type-confusion potential in a memory-safety-focused language; the only thing keeping it from critical is that it requires the non-default `--tree-walk` mode and is a read rather than a write. || [high/high] Verified against actual code, not the snippet. src/eval.c:9740-9742 reads exactly: `if (rest_idx >= 0 && (int)k > rest_idx) arr_idx = arr_len - (pat_count - 1 - k); else arr_idx = k; LatValue *elem = &scr.value.as.array.elems[arr_idx];`

Mechanism re-derived for `[first, ...mid, last]` against `[10,20,30,40]`: pat_count=3, rest_idx=1 (the loop at 9692-9695 sets rest_idx to the rest element, fixed_count=2), arr_len=4. For the trailing element k=2 (the general last post-rest element k=pat_count-1): the branch is taken (k>rest_idx) and arr_idx = arr_len - (pat_count-1-k) = 4 - (3-1-2) = 4 - 0 = 4. Valid indices are 0..3, so arr[4] is one past the end. The correct formula is arr_len - (pat_count - k) = 4 - 1 = 3 (last should bind arr[3]=40); the code is off by one too high for every post-rest element. Intermediate post-rest elements (e.g. b in `[a,...mid,b,c]`) read the wrong-but-in-bounds neighbor (silent mis-binding); the final post-rest element always reads exactly arr[arr_len].

The OOB is a genuine heap overflow, not a benign over-allocation read: value_array (src/value.c:206-214) sets cap = len<4 ? 4 : len, so for len>=4 cap==len exactly and arr[arr_len] lies past the allocation. The garbage slot at 9742 is then fed to value_deep_clone (9752, PAT_BINDING) → value_clone_impl (src/value.c:444), which reads the garbage type/phase/region_id and switches on the garbage type tag, dereferencing union pointer members (str_val via lat_strdup at 463, array.elems/len/cap at 466-475, strct.name/field_names at 477-485) — a type-confused wild-pointer dereference. PAT_LITERAL (9764) feeds it to value_equal similarly. The parser (src/parser.c:1118-1150) accepts elements after a rest with no rejection, and this is the tree-walk PAT_ARRAY handler reached under --tree-walk.

Empirically reproduced with the in-repo clat_asan: ASan reports heap-buffer-overflow READ of size 4 located 4 bytes after a 288-byte region (288 = 4 * sizeof(LatValue)=72, i.e. arr[4] of a 4-element array) inside value_clone_impl, called from eval_expr_inner's match handler via call_fn. Matches the claim precisely. The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, type, or control flow. || [high/high] Confirmed real via both algebra and empirical ASan. In current src/eval.c:9740 the post-rest index formula is arr_idx = arr_len - (pat_count - 1 - k). The correct index for a fixed element at pattern position k>rest_idx is arr_len - pat_count + k (derived from the correct rest-collection logic at 9716-9723: rest_start=k, rest_len=arr_len-fixed_count). The code's value equals (arr_len - pat_count + k) + 1 — always one too high; for the trailing element k=pat_count-1 it reduces to arr_idx=arr_len, exactly one past the end for any length. The only guard (9697-9709) checks arr_len>=fixed_count, a minimum-length check that does not bound arr_idx. The OOB LatValue is then dereferenced at 9752 (value_deep_clone for PAT_BINDING) or 9764 (value_equal for PAT_LITERAL), reading its type tag and union pointers. Running the exact trigger `[first, ...mid, last] => print(last)` over classify([10,20,30,40]) under the in-repo clat_asan --tree-walk yields heap-buffer-overflow READ of size 4 located 4 bytes after a 288-byte region (index 4 of a 4-elem array, sizeof(LatValue)=72, cap==len so truly OOB), with stack value_clone_impl <- eval_expr_inner PAT_ARRAY handler — matching the claim exactly. No clone/bound/early-return refutes it. Path is tree-walk only (--tree-walk is non-default; default is bytecode VM), which slightly limits exposure but the bug is deterministic and source-triggerable, so high severity for an OOB heap read with type-confusion potential is justified.

confirmed.title: freeze(x) where <contract> reads a non-closure value through the closure union member and dispatches through wild pointers
confirmed.trigger: Tree-walker (clat --tree-walk) running a one-line program: `let x = freeze([1, 2, 3]) where [99]`. The parser (src/parser.c:1301) accepts ANY expression after `where` via parse_expr, but eval treats the result as a closure with no `cr.value.type == VAL_CLOSURE` check. Verified: SEGV under ASan in env_get<-call_closure (READ at 0x8, wild pointer). confirmed.title: Bytecode verifier performs no stack-balance analysis; pop()/OP_POP underflow the value stack on crafted .latc
confirmed.trigger: A crafted .latc file whose chunk contains more stack-consuming opcodes than producers, e.g. code = [OP_NIL][OP_POP][OP_POP]...(many OP_POP)...[OP_HALT]. chunk_verify() (src/chunk.c:789-833, invoked from chunk_deserialize at src/latc.c:603) only checks opcode lengths, operand index/type ranges, jump targets and a terminating final op — it never models stack depth — so the chunk passes verification. The top-level frame starts with stack_top == vm->stack (src/stackvm.c:3204-3212).
confirmed.consequence: Each surplus pop does `vm->stack_top--; value_free(vm->stack_top)` (OP_POP, src/stackvm.c:3404-3408), walking stack_top below stack[0] into the in-struct `frame_count`/`frames[512]` arrays and then past the ~122KB StackVM allocation. Memory below the stack is reinterpreted as a LatValue: live frame pointers (chunk/ip/slots/upvalues) whose low bytes land in the VAL_STR..VAL_BUFFER range cause value_free() to free a wild pointer (type confusion / arbitrary free), and continued underflow dereferences addresses below the allocation -> segfault. Reachable by running an attacker-supplied .latc, which the codebase explicitly treats as untrusted.

confirmed.title: Local-slot operand of OP_GET_LOCAL/OP_SET_LOCAL (and INC/DEC/INDEX/FIELD/FREEZE_VAR locals) is never bounds-checked by the verifier or the VM

confirmed.title: Use-after-free of LatRef cell (and its pthread mutex) in GETINDEX Ref-proxy branch when destination register aliases the Ref register
confirmed.consequence: Use-after-free of the 144-byte LatRef struct and use of an already-destroyed pthread mutex. On macOS the immediate read of the freed/destroyed mutex was benign-looking (libsystem is uninstrumented) but is undefined behavior; under a different allocator, heap pressure, or concurrent allocation the freed region can be reused before ref_unlock, yielding memory corruption or a crash. pthread_mutex_unlock on a destroyed mutex is itself UB. Control returns to GETINDEX and ref_unlock(ref) at regvm.c:3799 calls pthread_mutex_unlock(&r->lock) (value.c:393) on the freed/destroyed struct => use-after-free + UB on a destroyed mutex. I proved it by instrumenting ref_release/ref_unlock and running `fn go(){ let r=Ref::new([10,20,30]); r=r[0]; return r } print(go())` under --regvm: output showed `ref_release FREEING 0x60d000000110` immediately followed by `ref_unlock CALLED on 0x60d000000110` (identical pointer). The bug itself is unambiguously real and reachable from ordinary safe Lattice source. confirmed.title: regchunk_verify omits register-window bounds checks for ROP_CALL / ROP_PRINT / ROP_INVOKE* — OOB / UAF register reads from crafted .rlatc
confirmed.trigger: A crafted/corrupt `.rlatc` file (an untrusted input format run directly via `clat file.rlatc`; main.c:451 -> regchunk_load -> deserialize copies instructions verbatim, latc.c:765-773). The only gate is regchunk_verify. Its reg_verify_operands (src/regvm.c:7342-7466) applies RCK_WINDOW (base+count<=256) ONLY to ROP_NEWARRAY/NEWTUPLE/NEWSTRUCT/NEWENUM/FREEZE_EXCEPT; ROP_CALL, ROP_PRINT, and the ROP_INVOKE family hit `default: break` (line 7466) with NO window validation. Example: a function that calls fn() which writes a non-interned heap value (e.g. ROP_NEWARRAY) into register 100 (its max_reg>=101) and returns — leaving reg_stack[256+100] as a freed-but-dangling VAL_ARRAY (value_free on a REGION_NONE value does not nil the slot; value.c:1513-1517) — then executes `ROP_PRINT A=255 B=102`. Since A/B are uint8_t and each frame window is exactly REGVM_REG_MAX=256 slots, R[a+i] reaches reg_stack[base+509], far past the 256-slot window, into the returned frame's window holding the dangling array. value_print then walks the freed elements. Equivalently ROP_CALL (R[a+1+i], src/regvm.c:4035 and 4125) and ROP_INVOKE (invoke_args=&R[args_base], src/regvm.c:4419-4420) read/clone caller registers past the frame window. At call depth ~63 (reachable via crafted recursion, frame_count up to REGVM_FRAMES_MAX=64) the index even exceeds the whole reg_stack[16384] array.
confirmed.consequence: Out-of-bounds / use-after-free read of LatValue slots that the VM then dereferences (value_print / rvm_clone / rvm_clone_or_borrow follow str_val/array.elems/struct pointers). Yields crashes, heap info-disclosure of adjacent (and at deep frames, out-of-array) memory, and reads of freed heap. A verifier bypass: defeats the security purpose of regchunk_verify for untrusted bytecode.

The comment there states the verifier is what protects the dispatch loop "which trusts every operand." So a crafted .rlatc reaches the dispatch loop iff it passes regchunk_verify.

ROP_PRINT and ROP_CALL do not appear in the switch at all (hit default: break, 7466). ROP_INVOKE/INVOKE_GLOBAL/INVOKE_LOCAL appear but only do RCK_STR on the method-name constant (7398-7424) — no window check on args_base+argc. The verifier also never references max_reg, so there is no per-register bound either. 3. NO RUNTIME CLAMP. Each frame gets exactly REGVM_REG_MAX=256 slots (regvm.h:14) carved from the fixed array LatValue reg_stack[256*64=16384] (regvm.h:95). A/B/C are uint8_t. confirmed.title: Constant-index opcodes lacking a 16-bit variant (OP_GET_FIELD/OP_SET_FIELD/OP_INVOKE/OP_BUILD_STRUCT/OP_BUILD_ENUM/OP_IMPORT) emit (uint8_t)idx with no overflow check -> VM dereferences a non-string constant as char*
confirmed.trigger: Any field access (`obj.field`), generic method invoke (`x.foo()` on a non-local/non-global receiver, or the desugared match-pattern invokes such as .len()/.payload()/.enum_name()), struct literal, enum-variant construction, or import whose name-string constant index lands >255. Reachable by placing >256 distinct constants in one chunk before the construct, e.g. `let s = "a0"; ...(>256 distinct string/big-int constants)...; let v = obj.lateField;` where `lateField`'s string constant gets index >=256, truncated to a byte that aliases an earlier non-string constant. These opcodes have NO 16-bit form and no guard (contrast OP_INVOKE_LOCAL/OP_INVOKE_GLOBAL at lines 869/905 which do check >255).
confirmed.consequence: The VM reads `frame->chunk->constants[idx].as.str_val` as the field/method/struct/path name (src/stackvm.c:4883 OP_GET_FIELD, :5082 OP_INVOKE, :4568/:4578 OP_BUILD_STRUCT which also reads name_idx+1..name_idx+field_count as field-name strings) and immediately passes it to intern()/strcmp(). If the aliased constant is not VAL_STR, as.str_val is reinterpreted union bits (e.g. an int_val) used as a char* -> wild pointer read -> crash / out-of-bounds read.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] VERIFIED REAL — confirmed by code reading AND an empirical ASan crash. Compiler side: src/stackcompiler.c emits (uint8_t)idx with no overflow guard and no 16-bit opcode for OP_GET_FIELD (:843), OP_INVOKE (:931-933), OP_BUILD_STRUCT (:944-946), OP_BUILD_ENUM (:1655-1657), OP_SET_FIELD (:2382), OP_IMPORT (:2757). These bypass the emit_constant_idx helper (:104-114) that the rest of the codebase uses to switch to *_16 opcodes. include/stackopcode.h has no _16 variant for any of these six. Reachability is fully established: chunk_add_constant/_nodupe (src/chunk.c:109-154) impose no 255 cap (const_cap just doubles), and emit_constant_idx itself only errors at >65535 — the codebase deliberately supports up to 65535 constants per chunk. The finder's cited contrast guards at :869 and :905 (OP_INVOKE_LOCAL/GLOBAL check idx>255 and emit _16) confirm the developers knew the hazard but missed these paths. VM side derefs the truncated index as a string: stackvm.c:4568/4578 (OP_BUILD_STRUCT reads constants[name_idx].as.str_val and constants[name_idx+1+i].as.str_val), :4883 (OP_GET_FIELD passes constants[name_idx].as.str_val to intern). PoC: a plain .lat program with 320 distinct large-int constants (each >127 so they occupy real VAL_INT slots) followed by `struct P{x,y}` + `P{x:1,y:2}`. Under clat_asan it SEGVs: value_struct_vm -> lat_strdup -> _platform_strlen, reading address 0x420 = 1056, exactly value_int(1056) reinterpreted as char* (nodupe name_idx>255 truncated onto an early VAL_INT constant). This is the claimed type-confusion: union int_val used as char* -> wild pointer read. OP_BUILD_STRUCT/OP_BUILD_ENUM have no fast-path fallback, so every struct literal / enum-variant construction in such a chunk truncates; field access on globals/chained exprs/call-results hits the generic OP_GET_FIELD. Trigger needs no malformed bytecode — only a large-but-legal source chunk, reachable accidentally in generated code (anneal/self-host). Severity high is correct: it is a deterministic memory-safety violation but primarily a wild/OOB read (crash + potential info disclosure; BUILD_STRUCT base_const+i can also exceed const_len for an OOB array read), not clearly a write primitive, so not critical. || [high/high] Mechanism re-derived from source and confirmed at every link.

(1) Compiler emits truncated single-byte indices with NO guard, exactly as claimed:
- src/stackcompiler.c:842-843 OP_GET_FIELD: `size_t idx = chunk_add_constant(...); emit_bytes(OP_GET_FIELD, (uint8_t)idx, line);`
- :930-933 OP_INVOKE: `emit_byte((uint8_t)idx, line);`
- :943-946 OP_BUILD_STRUCT: `emit_byte((uint8_t)name_idx, line);` (plus consecutive field-name constants via chunk_add_constant_nodupe)
- :1653-1657 OP_BUILD_ENUM
- :2380-2382 OP_SET_FIELD
- :2756-2757 OP_IMPORT
Also OP_GET_FIELD_LOCAL (:831) and OP_FREEZE_FIELD (:1673) share the flaw. By direct contrast, OP_INVOKE_LOCAL (:869 `if (idx > 255)`) and OP_INVOKE_GLOBAL (:905 `if (name_idx > 255 || method_idx > 255)`) DO guard and emit 16-bit variants — proving the omission is a real, recognized hazard, not intentional.

(2) Reachability is genuine, not blocked by any cap. chunk_add_constant (src/chunk.c:109) dedups strings/ints/floats but otherwise grows the pool; the only global limit is `too many constants in one chunk (>65535)` at stackcompiler.c:112, and that check lives only in emit_constant_idx (used by OP_CONSTANT/GET_GLOBAL/etc., which have 16-bit forms). The field opcodes bypass emit_constant_idx entirely, so a chunk with 256+ distinct constants (fully supported — that's why 16-bit opcodes exist) followed by a field access/invoke/struct-lit/enum/import whose name string lands at index >=256 truncates `(uint8_t)idx` to idx&0xff, aliasing an earlier constant.

(3) VM dereferences the aliased constant with no type check. src/stackvm.c:4883 (OP_GET_FIELD) `const char *field_name = frame->chunk->constants[name_idx].as.str_val; intern(field_name);`; :5082 (OP_INVOKE) reads `.as.str_val` and passes to stackvm_invoke_builtin; :4568/:4576-4578 (OP_BUILD_STRUCT) reads name_idx and name_idx+1..+field_count as `.as.str_val`. The LatValue union (include/value.h:55-60) overlaps `int64_t int_val` with `char *str_val`, so an aliased non-string constant yields a bogus char*. intern() (src/intern.c:67-85) immediately calls intern_hash(s)/strcmp(s)/strdup(s), all of which dereference s → wild-pointer read / OOB / crash.
