The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, types, or control flow. Severity high (not critical): triggering needs 256+ distinct constants in a single chunk — large but achievable in real/generated code with no malformed input — and Lattice generally executes trusted source, so it is more a robustness/correctness-under-scale and self-inflicted memory-safety defect than a remote attack surface. Confidence high. || [high/high] CONFIRMED and reproduced with a live SIGSEGV. The cited codegen sites in src/stackcompiler.c emit the name-string constant index as a raw (uint8_t)idx with no >255 check and no 16-bit fallback: line 843 (OP_GET_FIELD), 931-933 (OP_INVOKE), 944-946 (OP_BUILD_STRUCT), 1655-1657 (OP_BUILD_ENUM), 2380-2382 (OP_SET_FIELD), 2756-2757 (OP_IMPORT). I tried to refute by looking for (a) a per-chunk constant cap, (b) a compile-time error, (c) dedup keeping name strings at low indices, (d) a VM-side type guard, (e) 16-bit variants — none exist. Instead I found confirming evidence: the SAME function guards OP_INVOKE_LOCAL (line 869) and OP_INVOKE_GLOBAL (line 905) with explicit `if (idx > 255)` emitting OP_INVOKE_LOCAL_16/OP_INVOKE_GLOBAL_16, and include/stackopcode.h shows 16-bit variants exist ONLY for those two opcodes. emit_constant_idx (stackcompiler.c:104-113) explicitly supports up to 65535 constants/chunk (errors only above 65535), and chunk_add_constant (chunk.c:109) dedups + grows unboundedly, so a chunk with 256-65535 constants is a supported, reachable state. The VM reads constants[idx].as.str_val with no type check and passes it straight to intern()/snprintf("%s",...)/value_struct_vm/stackvm_invoke_builtin (stackvm.c:4568/4578, 4883, 4636-4637, 5082); when the truncated index aliases a VAL_INT, as.str_val is the int bits used as char* -> wild deref. Reproduction (default bytecode VM, freshly built clat): a top-level chunk with 300 distinct large-int constants (indices 0..255 filled with VAL_INT) followed by `let p = P { lateField: 5 }` crashes with SIGSEGV (exit 139) before any output. Controls isolate the cause: 300 ints with NO struct construct exits 0; 5 ints WITH the struct construct exits 0; only 300-ints + struct-build crashes. This is a deterministic memory-safety / wild-pointer-dereference bug triggerable from ordinary valid Lattice source (any large function exceeding 256 distinct constants before a field access / method invoke / struct or enum construction / import). Category type-confusion is accurate; severity high is appropriate for a reachable memory-safety crash in the language VM. (Note: OP_GET_FIELD_LOCAL at line 831 shares the same defect though it was not enumerated.)

confirmed.title: scope/spawn/select sub-chunk constant indices truncated to 8 bits → regvm executes wrong RegChunk / drops body / casts non-closure native_fn
confirmed.code: uint8_t body_idx = (uint8_t)add_regchunk_constant(spawn_ch);
confirmed.trigger: `clat --regvm` on a program with >256 constants before a `scope`/`spawn`/`select`. PoC 1 (body silently dropped): 300 distinct float locals then `spawn { print(0) }` — the spawn body never runs (no `0` printed) because the truncated sync_idx points at a non-closure constant (native_fn==NULL → skipped). PoC 2 (wrong bytecode executed): 200 `fn fN(a:any){a+N}` definitions then `scope { spawn { print(7) } }` — regvm runs a user function body as the spawn body, producing `cannot add immediate to Nil`.
confirmed.consequence: add_regchunk_constant returns a 16-bit constant index but ROP_SCOPE sync/spawn indices, ROP_SELECT chan_idx/body_idx (lines 2534, 2551, 2595, 2598, 2603) and binding_idx (2607) are all stored in 8-bit operand slots. The regvm reads `frame->chunk->constants[idx].as.closure.native_fn` and casts to RegChunk* with NO type check (regvm.c:5793/5816/5836/5942/5964/6039) and `.as.str_val` for the bind name (regvm.c:6032). With >256 constants the index is truncated, so the VM either silently skips the concurrency body, executes a completely different (attacker-influenceable) RegChunk, or — if the aliased constant carries a non-null pointer at the native_fn/str_val offset — dereferences a wild RegChunk*/char* pointer.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] VERIFIED REACHABLE and reproduced. The cited code is accurate: src/regcompiler.c:2499 `uint8_t body_idx = (uint8_t)add_regchunk_constant(spawn_ch);` (and the parallel sites 2534/2551 for ROP_SCOPE sync/spawn indices, 2595/2598/2603/2607 for ROP_SELECT). add_regchunk_constant (regcompiler.c:387) returns a uint16_t via add_constant (line 228), which only errors at REGVM_CONST_MAX = 65536 (include/regvm.h:16). regchunk_add_constant (regvm.c:126) dedups int/float/string by value, so N distinct literals yield N distinct constants. compile_reg_sub_body (line 333) saves rc, compiles the sub-body into a fresh sub-chunk, then restores `rc = saved`, so add_regchunk_constant appends the closure constant into the PARENT (top-level) chunk that already holds the prior constants — index >255 is genuinely produced and truncated to 8 bits.

No upstream guard prevents this. LOADK uses emit_ABx with a uint16_t Bx field (regcompiler.c:177), so the compiler intentionally supports >256 constants per chunk for normal access; only the concurrency sub-chunk indices are crammed into 8-bit ABC operand slots, making the truncation an asymmetric defect rather than a universal limit that would block such programs.

The decode side confirms the type confusion: regvm.c:5793/5816/5836 read `frame->chunk->constants[idx].as.closure.native_fn` and cast to RegChunk* with no VAL_CLOSURE/type check, indices coming from 8-bit REG_GET_B/REG_GET_C operands.

I built clat (make clean && make clat) and reproduced with clat --regvm: (1) `spawn { print(0) }` alone prints 0 correctly; (2) 300 distinct float locals + `spawn { print(0) }` prints NOTHING, exit 0 — body silently dropped (truncated index lands on a non-closure constant, native_fn==NULL → skipped); (3) 200 `fn fN(a:any){a+N}` defs + `scope { spawn { print(7) } }` yields `regvm error: cannot add immediate to Nil` — the truncated index aliases a different RegChunk (a user-function body) which is then executed as the spawn body. Both stated PoCs match exactly.

Impact: reachable from plain attacker-controlled .lat source via `clat --regvm` with no special privileges. Demonstrated manifestations are silent dropped concurrency bodies (silent incorrectness) and execution of a wrong, source-influenceable RegChunk. The further wild-pointer deref (when the aliased constant is a VAL_STR whose as.str_val overlaps as.closure.native_fn in the union → char* cast to RegChunk* and dereferenced by regvm_run_sub) is a genuine type confusion enabled by the missing type check, though my PoCs surfaced the silent-drop and wrong-chunk variants rather than a hard crash. Severity high is correct: real type-confusion/soundness hole in the regvm backend reachable from source, but typically surfaces as silent wrong results or a caught runtime error rather than guaranteed memory corruption, so not critical. || [high/high] Mechanism verified in actual code AND reproduced dynamically. add_regchunk_constant (src/regcompiler.c:387-396) returns uint16_t via add_constant, which permits constant indices up to REGVM_CONST_MAX=65535 (lines 228-236) and only errors at >65535 — there is no 256 guard. Lines 2499/2534/2551/2595/2598/2603/2607 all cast that 16-bit index to uint8_t and pack it into the 8-bit A/B/C operand slots of a 32-bit RegInstr (emit_ABC/REG_ENCODE_ABC). The constant table is a single per-chunk table (regchunk_add_constant, regvm.c:126-155) shared by deduplicated float/int/string literals and sub-chunk closures, so 300 distinct float literals fill indices 0..299 and the spawn sub-chunk lands at index 300 -> truncated to 44. On the VM side regvm.c:5793/5816/5836/5942/5964/6039/6062 cast frame->chunk->constants[idx].as.closure.native_fn to RegChunk* with NO .type==VAL_CLOSURE check, and 6032 reads .as.str_val; the union layout (include/value.h:55-124) puts closure.native_fn at offset ~48 vs float_val/str_val at offset 0, so an aliased float yields native_fn==NULL (body silently dropped via the partial if(sync_body) guard) and an aliased compiled-function closure yields a valid-but-WRONG RegChunk* that is executed. I reproduced both PoCs: 300 distinct floats + spawn{print(0)} printed nothing under --regvm while the 5-constant control and --tree-walk both printed 0; 200 fns + scope{spawn{print(7)}} produced 'regvm error: cannot add immediate to Nil' (a user fn body run as the spawn body) while the 3-fn control printed 7. The default bytecode VM also SIGSEGV'd (exit 139) on PoC1. One refinement to the finder's claim: the truncated index (0..255) is always < const_len (>256), so the constants[] array read itself is in-bounds — there is no out-of-bounds array access; the demonstrated impact is type confusion / wrong-code execution / dropped concurrency body, and a fully wild RegChunk*/char* deref under regvm is situational (depends on the aliased constant and the partial null guards). Even so, casting a non-closure union member to RegChunk* with no type check and then executing it as bytecode, triggerable by a valid program with >256 distinct constants before a scope/spawn/select, is a genuine high-severity type-confusion/correctness-and-safety bug. || [high/high] Confirmed real and empirically reproduced. add_regchunk_constant (src/regcompiler.c:387) returns a 16-bit index from add_constant, which permits up to REGVM_CONST_MAX=65535 constants; regchunk_add_constant (src/regvm.c:126) dedups floats/ints/strings by value so distinct constants genuinely accumulate. At regcompiler.c:2499/2534/2551/2595/2598/2603/2607 that 16-bit index is cast straight to uint8_t and packed into ROP_SCOPE/ROP_SELECT data words with NO guard rejecting indices >255 (the only constant-count check, line 231-233, is the 65535 limit). The sub-chunk constants are added to the PARENT chunk's table (compile_reg_sub_body restores rc to the parent before add_regchunk_constant runs), so when the script has >256 prior constants the index truncates. The regvm reads frame->chunk->constants[idx].as.closure.native_fn and casts to RegChunk* with no type/tag check (regvm.c:5793/5816/5836 and the ROP_SELECT paths). value_float zero-inits the union, so a truncated index hitting a float constant gives native_fn==NULL -> body silently skipped; an index hitting a user-fn closure constant gives a valid-but-wrong RegChunk* that gets executed; a constant with non-null garbage at the native_fn/str_val offset would be dereferenced as a wild pointer (genuine type confusion). I built clat and ran both PoCs: PoC1 (300 distinct float locals + spawn{print(0)}) prints nothing under --regvm vs '0' under --tree-walk (body dropped); PoC2 (200 fns + scope{spawn{print(7)}}) errors 'cannot add immediate to Nil' under --regvm vs '7' under --tree-walk (wrong RegChunk executed). No clone, bound, null-guard, or fallback refutes it. Severity high (not critical): triggered by ordinary valid source, causes silent concurrency-body loss and wrong-bytecode execution, with a plausible but not trivially-weaponized path to wild-pointer dereference/memory corruption when the VM runs untrusted Lattice code.

confirmed.title: Invalid free() of uninitialized pointer in interpolated-string error recovery
confirmed.code:
interp_fail:
        for (size_t i = 0; i <= count; i++) free(parts[i]);
confirmed.trigger: Any Lattice source containing an interpolation whose body is not a single complete expression, e.g. the one-line program: print("${1 2}")  (lexer emits INTERP_START, INT(1), INT(2), INTERP_END).
confirmed.consequence: In parse_primary's TOK_INTERP_START loop, parts[] is allocated with malloc (uninitialized). Each iteration sets exprs[count] and does count++, but parts[count] is only written in the TOK_INTERP_MID / TOK_INTERP_END branches. When the token after the parsed expression is neither MID nor END (e.g. a second expression), the else branch (lines 1258-1262) does `goto interp_fail` WITHOUT writing parts[count]. The cleanup loop `for i <= count` then calls free(parts[count]) on an uninitialized heap-garbage pointer. Confirmed under ASan: SEGV inside free() with freed pointer = 0xbebebebebebebe (ASan's uninitialized-heap fill byte), i.e. a wild free / heap corruption, crashing the interpreter on every backend (the parser is shared frontend). Fix: initialize parts[count]=NULL before parsing each interp expr, or use `i < count` (since parts[0..count-1] are the only ones reliably set when failing mid-segment).
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] VERIFIED REAL AND REACHABLE — confirmed by reading the source and by empirical ASan repro.

Code (src/parser.c:1226-1271): In parse_primary's TOK_INTERP_START branch, `parts = malloc((cap+1)*sizeof(char*))` is uninitialized. `parts[0]` is set before the loop. Inside the loop, after `parse_expr` succeeds, `exprs[count]=e; count++` runs FIRST (lines 1248-1249), and `parts[count]` is written ONLY in the TOK_INTERP_MID branch (line 1253) or TOK_INTERP_END branch (line 1256). The else branch (lines 1258-1262) sets *err and `goto interp_fail` WITHOUT writing `parts[count]`. The cleanup at 1266-1267 is `for (size_t i = 0; i <= count; i++) free(parts[i])` — so it frees `parts[count]`, which in the else-branch failure is never initialized. (The `if(!e) goto interp_fail` path at 1242 is safe because count is not yet incremented and parts[count] was set by the prior MID branch; the bug is specific to the else branch after count++.)

REACHABILITY (no upstream guard): The lexer (src/lexer.c:464-547, lex_string_or_interp) tokenizes `"${1 2}"` with no single-expression validation: it emits TOK_INTERP_START, then lex_one repeatedly for `1` and `2` (neither LBRACE/RBRACE, so brace depth stays 1), then on `}` breaks and on `"` emits TOK_INTERP_END. Token stream: INTERP_START, INT(1), INT(2), INTERP_END — exactly as claimed. In the parser, parse_expr parses `1` and stops (INT_LIT `2` is not a binary operator/postfix, so it is left unconsumed); count becomes 1; peek is INT_LIT (not MID/END) → else branch → goto interp_fail with count=1 and parts[1] uninitialized. This is the shared frontend used by all three backends (tree-walk, bytecode VM, regvm), reachable from a trivial one-line program.

EMPIRICAL CONFIRMATION: Ran `print("${1 2}")` through ./clat_asan → AddressSanitizer SEGV on address 0x...bebebebebe inside __asan Deallocate / free (frame #1) called from parse_primary (frame #2), x1 register = 0xbebebebebebebebe (ASan malloc fill = uninitialized heap). This is a genuine invalid free of an uninitialized pointer — undefined behavior with heap-corruption potential in release builds, reliably triggerable from untrusted source. Severity high is appropriate (memory-safety / wild free). The finder's fix (init parts[count]=NULL before parsing, or use `i < count`) is correct. || [high/high] Verified against the actual code at src/parser.c:1226-1272 and reproduced under ASan.

Mechanism (re-derived from source):
- Line 1230: `char **parts = malloc((cap + 1) * sizeof(char *));` — uninitialized (malloc, not calloc). Same for the realloc-grown region at line 1245.
- Line 1237: `parts[0]` is written before the loop, so only index 0 is reliably valid at loop entry.
- In the loop body, after `parse_expr` succeeds, lines 1248-1249 do `exprs[count] = e; count++`, so `count >= 1` from here on. `parts[count]` is written ONLY inside the TOK_INTERP_MID branch (1253) or TOK_INTERP_END branch (1256).
- The else branch (1258-1262) is reached when the token following the parsed expression is neither MID nor END. It does `goto interp_fail` WITHOUT having written `parts[count]`. So at that point `parts[count]` (count>=1) is uninitialized heap garbage.
- Cleanup at 1266-1267: `for (size_t i = 0; i <= count; i++) free(parts[i]);` — the inclusive `<= count` bound frees `parts[count]`, i.e. the uninitialized slot → wild free.

Note the parse_expr-failure path (1242) is NOT affected: there count was not yet incremented, and parts[0..count] were all written by prior MID iterations, so `<= count` is safe there. The bug is specific to the else branch where count was just bumped past the last written part. The finder correctly identified inclusive-vs-exclusive bound + write-ordering; no misread of ownership/types.

Trigger confirmed: `print("${1 2}")`. parse_expr parses `1`, leaving INT(2) which is neither MID nor END → else branch with count=1, parts[1] uninitialized. ASan output: `SEGV ... in free ... in parse_primary` with freed pointer register x1 = 0xbebebebebebebebe (ASan's 0xbe malloc-fill for uninitialized heap), i.e. a wild free. Crash occurs in the shared parser frontend, so it affects all backends.

Severity: high is appropriate — attacker-controlled source triggers free() of an uninitialized pointer (undefined behavior / heap corruption primitive), reliably crashing the parser (DoS) on every backend; in a non-ASan build the freed slot is whatever stale heap content occupied the fresh allocation, which can be a controllable invalid-free. Fix as proposed (init parts[count]=NULL each iteration, or use `i < count`) is correct. || [high/high] Verified against src/parser.c:1226-1272 (parse_primary, TOK_INTERP_START branch). The code matches the finding: `parts` is allocated with malloc (line 1230, uninitialized), parts[0] is written (1237), and inside the loop exprs[count]=e then count++ (1248-1249) happens BEFORE parts[count] is written. parts[count] is only written in the MID branch (1253) or END branch (1256). The else branch (1258-1262) does `goto interp_fail` with count already incremented but parts[count] still uninitialized garbage. The cleanup loop at 1267 is `for (i=0; i<=count; i++) free(parts[i])`, so it free()s parts[count] = uninitialized heap value.

Invariant trace: at loop top parts[0..count] are valid, so the `if(!e) goto interp_fail` path (1242) is safe (count unchanged). Only the else-branch path is unsafe: after count++ the new parts[count] is not yet written. Bounds are fine (parts sized cap+1, count<=cap), so this is an uninitialized read of allocated heap, not OOB — i.e. a wild free of a garbage pointer.

I attempted to refute via: (a) a clone/null-init of parts — none exists (plain malloc, no calloc, no parts[count]=NULL before parsing); (b) an early return before the increment — none; (c) parse_expr consuming the stray token — it does not (it returns after the single literal). I could not refute it.

Reachability confirmed by execution: `print("${1 2}")` parses `1` as a complete expr, leaves token INT(2) which is neither MID nor END, taking the else branch. Running ./clat_asan on this input produces AddressSanitizer SEGV in free, called from parse_primary, with the freed pointer = 0xbebebebebebebebe (ASan's malloc-uninitialized fill byte 0xbe), proving free() is invoked on uninitialized parts[count]. This is attacker-controllable (any malformed interpolation body), in the shared parser frontend, so it crashes all backends. A wild free of an uninitialized pointer is a genuine memory-safety bug (heap corruption / DoS), so high severity is justified. Suggested fix matches: use `i < count` in the cleanup loop, or initialize parts[count]=NULL before each iteration.

confirmed.title: TOML array-of-tables grows a value_array buffer with libc realloc(), corrupting the heap allocator's tracking (double-free)
confirmed.trigger:
Parse a TOML document with 5+ `[[a]]` array-of-table headers (initial cap from value_array(NULL,0) is 4, so the 5th entry triggers the realloc). Reachable via toml_parse(...) under the tree-walk backend (clat --tree-walk) or inside a spawned scope/fiber in the bytecode VM — any context where g_heap is set. Minimal repro: toml_parse("[[a]]
x=1
[[a]]
x=2
[[a]]
x=3
[[a]]
x=4
[[a]]
x=5
"). ASan confirms: realloc in toml_ops_parse frees the block, then fluid_heap_free (via evaluator_free->dual_heap_free) frees the same pointer again.
confirmed.consequence: arr_val->as.array.elems was allocated by value_array() via lat_alloc(), which routes to fluid_alloc()/arena_alloc() when g_heap/g_arena is active (always set in the tree-walker and in spawned VM threads). fluid_alloc malloc()s the block AND records the pointer in the heap's tracking list. Calling libc realloc() on it frees the old block and returns a new untracked pointer, leaving a dangling FluidAlloc node pointing at the freed memory. When the heap is later swept/freed, free(a->ptr) double-frees that block (confirmed double-free under AddressSanitizer). For an arena (g_arena) the realloc would instead be applied to a bump-pointer that libc never allocated, an outright invalid-realloc/corruption. Attacker-controlled TOML input yields heap corruption.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] VERIFIED REAL and reachable. The cited code at src/toml_ops.c:454-463 matches the file exactly: it grows arr_val->as.array.elems with plain libc realloc() instead of the project's heap-aware lat_realloc_routed()/fluid_realloc().

Mechanism confirmed end-to-end:
1. The array buffer originates from value_array(NULL,0) (toml_ops.c:449) -> value.c:206-213: elems = lat_alloc(cap*...) with cap=4. lat_alloc (value.c:113-117) routes to fluid_alloc(g_heap->fluid,...) when g_heap is set. fluid_alloc (memory.c:41-49) malloc()s the block AND records a FluidAlloc node with a->ptr in h->allocs.
2. The buffer is stored into the map by lat_map_set (src/ds/hashmap.c) via a shallow memcpy(e->value, value, value_size) — NO deep-clone, NO heap detach — so the exact fluid-tracked pointer lives in the map, and lat_map_get returns a pointer to that inline LatValue (arr_val).
3. Five [[a]] headers all resolve to the same array key: entries fill len 0->4 (cap 4), and the 5th hits len(4) >= cap(4), invoking plain realloc() at line 459. libc realloc frees the original fluid-tracked block and returns a new untracked block; the FluidAlloc node still points at the freed memory.
4. At teardown evaluator_free -> dual_heap_free -> fluid_heap_free (memory.c:29-34) walks h->allocs and free(a->ptr)s the stale node = DOUBLE FREE. (The correct path fluid_realloc, memory.c:77-92, would have removed the old node — but toml_ops.c bypasses it.)

Reachability confirmed with NO upstream guard:
- Tree-walk backend: evaluator_new sets value_set_heap(ev->heap) (eval.c:13678) for the whole run; the toml_parse builtin (eval.c:5678-5689) calls toml_ops_parse with g_heap live. So `clat --tree-walk file.lat` where file.lat does toml_parse("[[a]]\nx=1\n[[a]]...[[a]]\n" with 5+ tables) triggers it directly on attacker-controlled input.
- Bytecode VM: spawned scope/fiber async tasks set value_set_heap(dual_heap_new()) (runtime.c:2471/2506); native_toml_parse (runtime.c:1323-1335) inside such a fiber triggers it too.
- The only non-triggering case is the DEFAULT bytecode VM main thread, where g_heap is NULL so value_array uses malloc and plain realloc is harmless. This narrows blast radius but does not refute the bug, since --tree-walk is a documented, tested backend (make test-tree-walk) and concurrency scopes are normal usage.
- parts[32] limits only dotted-key depth per header, not the number of [[a]] headers; the parse loop imposes no cap, so 5+ headers is freely accepted.

Severity: a heap double-free / allocator-tracking corruption driven by attacker-controlled TOML (config/network data) is a genuine memory-safety vulnerability. Kept at high (not critical) because the default bytecode main-thread path is immune; reachable via the tree-walk backend or any spawned concurrency context. || [high/high] Mechanism verified against the actual code. toml_ops.c:449 creates the array via value_array(NULL,0); value.c:206-213 forces cap = (len<4?4:len) so the elems buffer is allocated by lat_alloc(4*sizeof(LatValue)). value.c:113-117 shows lat_alloc routes to fluid_alloc(g_heap->fluid,...) whenever g_heap is set and g_arena is NULL, and memory.c:41-59 shows fluid_alloc both malloc()s the block AND records a FluidAlloc tracking node (a->ptr=ptr) in h->allocs. toml_ops.c:455-459 then grows this tracked buffer with bare libc realloc() (confirmed: no #define realloc, only <stdlib.h>; it is NOT lat_realloc_routed). realloc frees the old block (and moves it — always under ASan) and returns a new untracked pointer, leaving a stale FluidAlloc node pointing at freed memory. At teardown fluid_heap_free (memory.c:29-38) iterates h->allocs and free(a->ptr) on that stale node → double-free. The codebase itself documents exactly this hazard at value.c:119-126 and provides lat_realloc_routed as the correct API, which toml_ops.c neglects to use. Bounds are correct: cap starts at 4 even for an empty array, so the 5th [[a]] header (len=4>=cap=4) triggers the realloc — the finder's '5+ [[a]]' trigger is accurate. Reachability is genuine in two supported modes: tree-walk (eval.c:5685 calls toml_ops_parse with g_heap=ev->heap set at eval.c:13678, torn down via evaluator_free→dual_heap_free→fluid_heap_free), and the default bytecode VM inside a spawn{} fiber (stackvm.c:968-970 sets a thread-local DualHeap). No misread of ownership (the buffer is genuinely fluid-heap-owned, not borrowed), type (correct union member as.array.elems), or control flow. This is a real attacker-controllable double-free / heap corruption; high severity is appropriate for memory corruption from untrusted TOML input. || [high/high] Confirmed real after reading the actual code. toml_ops.c:455-463 grows arr_val->as.array.elems with bare libc realloc(). That buffer originates from value_array(NULL,0) (toml_ops.c:449), and value_array (value.c:206-214) allocates elems via lat_alloc(), which (value.c:113-117) routes to fluid_alloc(g_heap->fluid,...) whenever g_heap is set. fluid_alloc (memory.c:41-60) malloc()s the block AND records the pointer in a FluidAlloc tracking node. g_heap is set in the tree-walker (evaluator_new: ev->heap=dual_heap_new() at eval.c:13651, value_set_heap at 13678) for the duration of the toml_ops_parse native call (eval.c:5685; toml_ops_parse never saves/clears the heap), and in spawned bytecode-VM fibers (runtime.c:2470-2472). Trigger math holds: value_array(NULL,0) gives cap=4, so the 5th [[a]] (len==4>=cap==4) hits the realloc. libc realloc frees/relocates the old block and returns an untracked pointer (stored back into elems), but the FluidAlloc node still holds the now-dangling old pointer; at teardown fluid_heap_free (memory.c:29-39) does free(a->ptr) on it -> double-free (deterministic under ASan, which always relocates). The codebase corroborates the hazard: lat_realloc_routed (value.c:123-126) exists specifically to avoid this stale-pointer double-free, and eval.c:7710-7712 grows the identical as.array.elems buffer correctly via fluid_alloc+memcpy+fluid_dealloc; toml_ops.c:459 bypasses both. val_dealloc->lat_free (value.c:1492-1496) frees the new buffer separately, confirming the old tracked pointer is the one double-freed. No guard/clone/bounds-check/heap-save refutes it. Severity high is appropriate: attacker-controlled TOML (incl. package-manifest parsing at package.c:606/776) causes heap corruption. Minor scope caveat (does not lower validity): in the default bytecode VM main thread without spawn, g_heap is NULL so value_array uses plain malloc and realloc is self-consistent; the bug fires under --tree-walk or inside spawned fibers, exactly as the finding states.

confirmed.title: random_int(INT64_MIN, INT64_MAX): signed overflow makes range==0 -> division by zero (SIGFPE crash on x86-64/Windows)
confirmed.code:
uint64_t range = (uint64_t)(hi - lo) + 1;
    int64_t result = lo + (int64_t)((uint64_t)rand() % range);
confirmed.trigger: Lattice program: `random_int(0 - 9223372036854775807 - 1, 9223372036854775807)` (i.e. lo=INT64_MIN, hi=INT64_MAX). The `lo <= hi` guard at line 207 passes. `hi - lo` is computed in int64 and overflows (true diff 2^64-1), wrapping to bit pattern 0xFFFF...FF; (uint64_t)(...) = UINT64_MAX, +1 wraps to range==0. Then `rand() % range` divides by zero. Reproduced under the repo's UBSan binary (clat_asan): `src/math_ops.c:219:36 signed integer overflow` AND `src/math_ops.c:220:54 division by zero`.
confirmed.consequence: On x86-64 and Windows (the CI/release targets), integer modulo by zero raises SIGFPE and crashes the interpreter -> trivial denial-of-service from a one-line script. On arm64 the div-by-zero is defined to return 0 but the result is still garbage out-of-distribution. The `hi - lo` signed overflow is also UB the optimizer may exploit. No upstream guard prevents the full-range span.
confirmed.verifier_notes: [high/high] Verified against src/math_ops.c:200-221. The only upstream guard is `if (lo > hi)` at line 207; with lo=INT64_MIN, hi=INT64_MAX it passes (INT64_MIN <= INT64_MAX). Line 219 `(uint64_t)(hi - lo) + 1`: hi-lo is computed in int64_t (signed overflow, true diff 2^64-1 wraps to 0xFFFF...FF), cast to UINT64_MAX, +1 wraps to range==0; line 220 then does `rand() % 0`. Reachability fully confirmed: random_int is a registered 2-arg native (runtime.c:4255 rt_register_native, runtime.c:3911 mod_set_native, eval.c:4423), and native_random_int (runtime.c:732) forwards args[0]/args[1] to math_random_int with NO additional range validation. The literal 9223372036854775807 parses to exact INT64_MAX via strtoll (lexer.c:208), and `0 - 9223372036854775807 - 1` evaluates to INT64_MIN; nothing upstream clamps/rejects the full span. Reproduced directly under clat_asan: 'src/math_ops.c:219:36: signed integer overflow ... 9223372036854775807 - -9223372036854775808' AND 'src/math_ops.c:220:54: division by zero'. A normal call random_int(1,10) prints 8 on the release binary, proving the native is live in the default VM. On arm64 the release clat returns garbage (-9223372036046948711) since div-by-zero is defined there; on x86-64/Windows (the CI/release targets) integer modulo by zero raises SIGFPE -> interpreter crash, a trivial one-line DoS. Severity high (crash/DoS, no memory corruption/RCE). || [high/high] Mechanism re-derived from actual code and reproduced. src/math_ops.c:200-221 math_random_int: the ONLY guard (line 207 `if (lo > hi)`) checks low<=high and does nothing to bound the span, so lo=INT64_MIN, hi=INT64_MAX passes. Line 219 `uint64_t range = (uint64_t)(hi - lo) + 1;` computes hi-lo in int64: INT64_MAX-INT64_MIN = 2^64-1 overflows int64 (signed UB), wraps to bit pattern 0xFFFF...FF = -1; (uint64_t)(-1)=UINT64_MAX; +1 wraps to range==0 (verified deterministically with a standalone program: range=0). No clone/borrow/bound/type misread by the finder; the inclusive-range arithmetic and the missing full-span guard are exactly as described. Code at src/math_ops.c:200-222 matches the snippet; the ONLY guards are the VAL_INT type check (line 201) and `lo > hi` (line 207). INT64_MIN > INT64_MAX is false, so the full-range span passes. confirmed.title: native_load_bytecode casts active_vm to StackVM* without checking backend (SIGSEGV / memory unsafety under --regvm)
confirmed.trigger: Run any program with the register-VM backend: `clat --regvm prog.lat` where prog.lat does `let r = load_bytecode("inner.latc")` and inner.latc is a stack-VM .latc produced by `clat compile inner.lat -o inner.latc`. Under --regvm, current_rt->active_vm is a RegVM* (set in regvm_setup_dispatch, regvm.c:7193) but native_load_bytecode unconditionally casts it to StackVM* and calls stackvm_run on it. Sibling natives (native_lat_eval line 3403, native_require line 2958, native_async_iter line 2535) all guard on rt->backend first; this one does not (despite the comment 'can only run on the stack VM'). Verified: `clat --regvm loader.lat` exits 139 (SIGSEGV) while the stack VM exits 0.
confirmed.consequence: stackvm_run interprets a RegVM struct as a StackVM: it reads frame.ip/stack_top/stack from wrong offsets, dispatches whatever bytes lie there as opcodes, and writes through the misinterpreted stack pointer. Immediate result is a SIGSEGV crash (confirmed exit 139); because the stack interpreter is driven over a foreign struct and an attacker-supplied .latc, it is memory-unsafe (wild reads/writes), not merely a clean abort.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] VERIFIED REAL and reachable. Code at src/runtime.c:3762-3790 (native_load_bytecode) unconditionally casts current_rt->active_vm to StackVM* and calls stackvm_run, with NO backend check — only a `!current_rt->active_vm` null guard. The comment "can only run on the stack VM" is aspirational; nothing enforces it.

Reachability chain confirmed by reading the actual code:
1. The native is registered backend-agnostically: rt_register_native (runtime.c:4158) does env_define(rt->env, "load_bytecode", ...) at runtime.c:4305.
2. The register VM shares that env: regvm.c:178 `vm->env = rt->env;`, so load_bytecode is in scope under --regvm.
3. RegVM dispatches VM_NATIVE_MARKER closures by calling the native directly: regvm.c:4009-4010 `VMNativeFn native = (VMNativeFn)func->as.closure.native_fn;` (also 2840-2841).
4. Under --regvm, regvm_setup_dispatch (regvm.c:7191-7199) sets rt->backend = RT_BACKEND_REG_VM, rt->active_vm = vm (a RegVM*), and lat_runtime_set_current(vm->rt).
5. So native_load_bytecode casts a RegVM* to StackVM*. stackvm_run's prologue (stackvm.c:3180-3209) immediately does setjmp(vm->overflow_jmp), then `vm->rt->backend = ...; vm->rt->active_vm = vm;` (lines 3195-3196) — writing through vm->rt, which is read from the wrong offset of the RegVM struct (garbage pointer), plus reads vm->frames/frame_count/next_frame_slots at StackVM offsets. Classic type confusion → wild read/write.

Contrast with siblings, which all guard first: native_require branches on `current_rt->backend == RT_BACKEND_REG_VM` (runtime.c:2958), native_lat_eval likewise (3403), native_async_iter rejects regvm outright (2535-2536). This native is the lone unguarded one.

Reproduced end-to-end: built clat, `clat compile inner.lat -o inner.latc` (stack-VM .latc), loader.lat does `load_bytecode("inner.latc")`. Stack VM: exit 0. `clat --regvm loader.lat`: exit 139 (SIGSEGV). A trivial --regvm program (no load_bytecode) runs fine (prints 42, exit 0), isolating the cause. lldb backtrace: EXC_BAD_ACCESS (address=0x12c) in `stackvm_run + 196` — exactly the type-confusion path. Severity high (not critical): requires the user to opt into the --regvm backend and call load_bytecode with a loadable .latc; not a default/remote-data path. But it is genuine memory unsafety (wild writes through a misinterpreted struct, stack interpreter driven over a foreign struct with attacker-supplied bytecode), not a clean abort. Trivial fix: add the same `if (current_rt->backend == RT_BACKEND_REG_VM) return value_nil();` (or a proper regvm path) that the sibling natives already have. || [high/high] Verified against current source and reproduced empirically. runtime.c:3779 (native_load_bytecode) unconditionally does `StackVM *vm = (StackVM *)current_rt->active_vm;` then `stackvm_run(vm, ...)`, with no backend check. Under --regvm, regvm.c:7191-7193 (regvm_setup_dispatch) sets rt->backend=RT_BACKEND_REG_VM and rt->active_vm = the RegVM*. Sibling natives that the finder cited DO guard: native_require (runtime.c:2958) and native_lat_eval (runtime.c:3403) branch on RT_BACKEND_REG_VM and only cast to StackVM* on the stack path; load_bytecode omits this. The native is reachable from regvm: it is registered into the shared runtime registry (runtime.c:4305) and the register VM invokes natives generically (regvm.c:4010 VMNativeFn). The two structs are layout-incompatible: StackVM (stackvm.h:57-98) has StackCallFrame frames[], size_t frame_count, LatValue stack[], stack_top, with rt and overflow_jmp at large offsets; RegVM (regvm.h:92-118) has RegCallFrame frames[], int frame_count, reg_stack[]/reg_stack_top, rt at a different offset. stackvm_run (stackvm.c:3180+) immediately setjmp(vm->overflow_jmp), writes vm->rt->backend, and writes through vm->frames[vm->frame_count++]->chunk — all at offsets that point into unrelated/wrong RegVM memory. Empirical repro with clat_asan: `clat_asan --regvm loader.lat` (loader calls load_bytecode on a valid stack .latc) produced UBSan 'member access within null pointer of type LatRuntime' (the vm->rt deref) and AddressSanitizer 'SEGV ... caused by a WRITE memory access' at 0x12c, with backtrace stackvm_run <- native_load_bytecode <- regvm_dispatch <- regvm_run <- main; the stack-VM path ran load_bytecode without crashing. This is a genuine CWE-843 type confusion causing a wild write, not a clean abort. Severity high (not critical) because it requires the user to opt into the non-default --regvm backend and call load_bytecode; it is a local memory-safety crash, not a default-config remote exploit. || [high/high] Confirmed against the actual source. native_load_bytecode (src/runtime.c:3762-3790) has only three early-return guards: arg-count/type, chunk_load failure, and !current_rt->active_vm. It then does StackVM *vm = (StackVM *)current_rt->active_vm; stackvm_run(vm, chunk, &result) (lines 3779-3781) with NO check on current_rt->backend. Sibling natives explicitly guard: native_require (line 2958) and native_lat_eval (line 3403) branch on `current_rt->backend == RT_BACKEND_REG_VM` to use RegVM*/regvm_run vs StackVM*/stackvm_run, and native_async_iter (lines 2535-2536) rejects non-stack backends entirely — establishing the contract this function violates despite its own comment. load_bytecode is registered unconditionally in lat_runtime_init (line 4305), so it is reachable in both backends. Under --regvm, regvm_setup_dispatch (src/regvm.c:7191-7193) sets backend=RT_BACKEND_REG_VM and active_vm=vm (a RegVM*). StackVM (include/stackvm.h:98) and RegVM (include/regvm.h:92-118) are distinct structs with completely different leading layouts (StackVM starts with `LatValue stack[]; LatValue *stack_top; Env *env`; RegVM starts with `RegCallFrame frames[]; int frame_count; LatValue reg_stack[]; size_t reg_stack_top; Env *env`), so stackvm_run reads ip/stack_top/env/error/rt from wrong offsets and writes through a misinterpreted stack pointer — genuine type confusion with wild reads/writes, plus a wrong-offset free(vm->error) at line 3785. I could find no clone, bound, null check, early return, or invariant that prevents this; the cast is unconditional and the path is trivially reachable from user code (`load_bytecode("x.latc")` under --regvm). Downgraded from critical to high only because it is gated behind the opt-in --regvm backend; it is real memory unsafety, not a clean abort.

confirmed.title: native_breakpoint casts active_vm to StackVM* without checking backend (SIGSEGV under --regvm)
confirmed.code: StackVM *vm = (StackVM *)rt->active_vm;
confirmed.trigger: Run with the register-VM backend: `clat --regvm prog.lat` where prog.lat calls `breakpoint()` and then any expression is entered at the (breakpoint) prompt (e.g. piping `printf 'x + 1\ncont\n'`). breakpoint() has no rt->backend guard, so under --regvm it treats the RegVM* active_vm as a StackVM*: it reads vm->frame_count / vm->frames[...] / vm->stack_top from wrong offsets and, for any entered expression, calls stackvm_run(vm, chunk, &result) at line 3603 with the RegVM pointer. Verified: feeding an expression yields exit 139 (SIGSEGV).
confirmed.consequence: Type-confused reads of StackVM fields off a RegVM struct, followed by stackvm_run executing over the RegVM memory, causing SIGSEGV (confirmed exit 139) and memory-unsafe behavior. The interactive debugger builtin is unusable and crash-prone on the register VM backend.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] Verified against source AND reproduced empirically. The path is genuinely reachable with no upstream guard:

1. `breakpoint` is registered UNCONDITIONALLY in `lat_runtime_init` (src/runtime.c:4477) into `rt->env`, which the RegVM shares (`regvm_init` sets `vm->env = rt->env`, src/regvm.c:178). So a `.lat` program calling `breakpoint()` resolves the global.
2. The RegVM dispatches native closures via `VMNativeFn native = (VMNativeFn)func->as.closure.native_fn;` when `default_values == VM_NATIVE_MARKER` (src/regvm.c:4009-4010), so under `--regvm` the call routes to `native_breakpoint`.
3. Before user code runs, `regvm_run`→`regvm_setup_dispatch` sets `vm->rt->backend = RT_BACKEND_REG_VM` and `vm->rt->active_vm = vm` (the RegVM*) at src/regvm.c:7192-7193.
4. `native_breakpoint` (src/runtime.c:3514-3521) does NOT branch on `rt->backend` — it unconditionally does `StackVM *vm = (StackVM *)rt->active_vm;`. This contradicts the codebase's own convention: sibling natives at src/runtime.c:2958 and 3403 explicitly check `current_rt->backend == RT_BACKEND_REG_VM` before casting to `RegVM *`. The guard is simply missing here. It then reads `vm->frame_count`/`vm->frames[...]` at wrong offsets and, for any entered expression, calls `stackvm_run(vm, chunk, &result)` (line 3603) passing the RegVM* where a StackVM* is expected.

Empirical repro (clat_asan, `printf 'x + 1\ncont\n' | ./clat_asan --regvm bp.lat`): UBSan reports `member access within null pointer of type 'LatRuntime'` at stackvm.c:3085 (vm->rt read from wrong offset), then ASan reports `SEGV ... caused by a WRITE memory access` in `stackvm_run+0x15b0`, call chain `regvm_run → regvm_dispatch → native_breakpoint → stackvm_run`. This exactly matches the claimed exit-139 SIGSEGV / type-confusion with wild writes.

Severity corrected from high to medium: it is a genuine, trivially-reproducible memory-safety type confusion (confirmed wild write), but the trigger is self-inflicted — it requires the developer to (a) explicitly insert the `breakpoint()` debugging builtin, (b) run on the non-default `--regvm` backend, and (c) provide interactive input. It is not reachable from ordinary/untrusted program data and the blast radius is the developer's own debugging session, so it does not rise to high in practical impact. The fix is a one-line backend guard mirroring lines 2958/3403 (either dispatch to RegVM fields or no-op breakpoint under RT_BACKEND_REG_VM). || [high/high] Mechanism re-derived from source and confirmed by reproduction + lldb backtrace.

CODE: src/runtime.c:3521 `StackVM *vm = (StackVM *)rt->active_vm;` inside native_breakpoint has NO backend guard. It then reads vm->frame_count / vm->frames[...] (3525-3526) and, for any entered expression, calls stackvm_run(vm, chunk, &result) at line 3603.

REACHABILITY: breakpoint() is registered as a SHARED native via rt_register_native(rt,"breakpoint",native_breakpoint,0) (runtime.c:4477), stored in rt->env. The register VM dispatches natives from the same closure->as.closure.native_fn (regvm.c:2841/4010), so breakpoint() invokes native_breakpoint under --regvm.

ACTIVE_VM TYPE: regvm_setup_dispatch (regvm.c:7192-7193) sets rt->backend = RT_BACKEND_REG_VM and rt->active_vm = (RegVM*)vm. So under --regvm, active_vm is genuinely a RegVM*, not a StackVM*.

LAYOUT MISMATCH: StackVM (stackvm.h:57-98) is {StackCallFrame frames[N]; size_t frame_count; LatValue stack[M]; LatValue *stack_top; ...}; RegVM (regvm.h:92-118) is {RegCallFrame frames[N]; int frame_count; LatValue reg_stack[...]; ...}. Different element sizes/counts, so vm->frame_count, vm->frames[], vm->stack_top read garbage offsets, and stackvm_run writes StackVM fields into RegVM memory.

CORRECT PATTERN EXISTS NEARBY: lat_eval (runtime.c:3403) and async_iter (runtime.c:2535) both branch on current_rt->backend == RT_BACKEND_REG_VM before casting; native_breakpoint omits this guard entirely.

EMPIRICAL CONFIRMATION: Built clat, ran `printf 'x + 1\ncont\n' | ./clat --regvm prog.lat` where prog.lat calls breakpoint(). Output prints native_breakpoint's "-- breakpoint() hit at line 0 --" (line 0 already reflects the misread RegVM frame) then exits 139 (SIGSEGV). Same program under the default stack VM correctly prints "=> 6". lldb backtrace: frame#3 regvm_run -> regvm dispatch -> native_breakpoint -> frame#0 stackvm_run+100, EXC_BAD_ACCESS (code=1, address=0x128) on `str wzr,[x0,#0x128]` — stackvm_run zeroing a StackVM field off the RegVM pointer. This is exactly the claimed type-confusion -> SIGSEGV / memory-unsafe write.

The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, or control flow; the bug is real and triggers deterministically. Severity: confirmed memory-safety crash (and out-of-struct writes via stackvm_run) in a shipped debugger builtin under a supported backend, so high is justified; only mild mitigation is that it requires the developer to use --regvm, include breakpoint() in source, and interactively enter an expression (not attacker-controlled input). || [high/medium] CONFIRMED real and empirically reproduced. native_breakpoint (src/runtime.c:3514-3626) does `StackVM *vm = (StackVM *)rt->active_vm;` at line 3521 with NO rt->backend guard — I read the entire function and `grep` over lines 3514-3626 finds zero references to `backend`/`RT_BACKEND`/`RegVM`. The native is registered unconditionally for every backend at src/runtime.c:4477 (`rt_register_native(rt, "breakpoint", native_breakpoint, 0)`). Under --regvm, regvm_setup_dispatch (src/regvm.c:7191-7193) sets `rt->backend = RT_BACKEND_REG_VM` and `rt->active_vm = vm` where vm is a RegVM*. The two structs have totally different layouts: StackVM (include/stackvm.h:57-98) is {StackCallFrame frames[]; size_t frame_count; LatValue stack[]; LatValue *stack_top; ...} whereas RegVM (include/regvm.h:92-118) is {RegCallFrame frames[]; int frame_count; LatValue reg_stack[]; size_t reg_stack_top; ...}. So `vm->frame_count` and `vm->frames[...]` at line 3525-3526 read from wrong offsets, and the entered-expression path calls `stackvm_run(vm, chunk, &result)` at line 3603 with the RegVM pointer, writing StackVM-shaped frame/stack state into RegVM memory. I built clat clean and reproduced exactly: under default stack VM the banner correctly shows "line 3"; under `--regvm` the same program prints "line 0" (the type-confused mis-read already visible), and `printf 'x + 1\ncont\n' | ./clat --regvm prog.lat` exits 139 (SIGSEGV), matching the finder's claim. No clone, bound, null-check, or early return refutes it. I downgrade severity from high to medium only because the trigger surface is narrow and developer-only: it requires opting into --regvm, an explicit breakpoint() call in source, and interactively typing an expression at the prompt — there is no untrusted/remote input vector; the impact is a crash plus memory corruption of an interactive debugging builtin the developer themselves invoked. The fix is a one-line `if (rt->backend != RT_BACKEND_STACK_VM) { ...graceful message...; return value_unit(); }` guard (or a RegVM equivalent of the mini-REPL).

confirmed.title: Path traversal / arbitrary file write via unsanitized registry-returned version string used as a filesystem path component
confirmed.code:
char *resolved = find_best_version(versions, ver_count, version);
...
char *cached = find_cached_package(name, resolved);   /* line 510: cache_dir/name/resolved */
...
snprintf(pkg_cache, sizeof(pkg_cache), "%s/%s/%s", cache_dir, name, resolved);   /* line 562 */
if (!registry_download_package(name, resolved, pkg_cache, err)) {   /* line 565: mkdir + write into pkg_cache */
confirmed.trigger: Declare a dependency with constraint "*" in lattice.toml (e.g. `evil = "*"`, which passes pkg_path_component_is_safe) and run `clat install` against an attacker-controlled, compromised, or MITM'd registry (LATTICE_REGISTRY, or the default if compromised). Have the registry answer GET /packages/evil/versions with a body like {"versions":["../../../../../../../../tmp/pwned"]}. registry_fetch_versions extracts the string verbatim (naive quote-matching), and because the constraint is "*" pkg_semver_satisfies() returns true unconditionally; find_best_version() returns the first element unchanged (the `!best` short-circuit at line 383 selects versions[0] regardless of whether it parses as semver). The returned `resolved` is never checked with pkg_path_component_is_safe() — unlike the manifest's own version field, which IS validated at line 677.
confirmed.consequence: `resolved` is used directly as a path component in find_cached_package (line 510) and pkg_cache (line 562). registry_download_package() then fs_mkdir()s `~/.lattice/packages/<name>/<resolved>` and builtin_write_file()s the attacker-controlled HTTP response body to <dir>/main.lat (and a generated lattice.toml). With `..` segments the path escapes the cache directory (verified: fs_mkdir is a single mkdir() the OS resolves through `..`, and the name subdir is pre-created so the climb resolves), giving the registry an arbitrary-directory file write with fully attacker-controlled content — e.g. planting or overwriting main.lat/lattice.toml anywhere the user can write (/tmp, ~/.config, other project trees). This is a supply-chain code-execution / file-clobber primitive that the surrounding code explicitly tries to prevent for manifest-supplied versions but fails to apply to registry-supplied versions.
confirmed.verifier_notes: [high/high] Verified the full chain in src/package.c against the actual file. The trigger is reachable from `clat install` with a benign-looking dependency `evil = "*"` (or `evil = {}`, which defaults version to "*" at lines 661/671). The manifest validation at line 677 only checks the CONSTRAINT string `d->version` with pkg_path_component_is_safe (lines 85-93); "*" passes trivially. registry_fetch_versions (lines 356-370) extracts version strings verbatim via strndup with zero validation. find_best_version (lines 379-389): for constraint "*", pkg_semver_satisfies returns true unconditionally at line 151 (short-circuits before parsing the version), and the `!best` branch at line 383 selects versions[0] verbatim even though "../../tmp/pwned" is not valid semver. The returned `resolved` is NEVER passed through pkg_path_component_is_safe — confirmed by reading lines 498-565; it flows directly into find_cached_package (line 510→237 snprintf), pkg_cache (line 562), and registry_download_package (line 565). fs_mkdir (fs_ops.c:100-112, native impl) is a single mkdir() that the kernel resolves through `..` segments; the name subdir is pre-created (lines 553-560) so the climb out of ~/.lattice/packages resolves through existing ancestors, and builtin_write_file (builtins.c:75-83) uses fopen("w") which likewise resolves `..`, writing attacker-controlled resp->body to <escaped_dir>/main.lat plus a generated lattice.toml. No upstream validator (fetch_package, lock handling, find_best_version, registry_download_package) sanitizes `resolved`. The finder correctly identified the asymmetry: the code explicitly guards manifest-supplied versions at line 677 but applies no equivalent check to registry-supplied versions. Constraint: requires a malicious/compromised/MITM'd registry (default is HTTPS cert-validated, so not a passive-network attack), but that is a recognized trust boundary the code itself warns about (lines 255-259), and the bug grants an arbitrary-directory file-write/clobber primitive with fully attacker-controlled content that escapes the intended cache sandbox — a distinct path-traversal vulnerability. Severity high is appropriate (arbitrary file write from network-controlled input), tempered slightly by the malicious-registry precondition but not negated by it. || [high/high] Mechanism verified end-to-end in the actual source. (1) fetch_from_registry (src/package.c:480-568): I read the full body — between find_best_version (line 498) and the uses of `resolved` as a filesystem path component in find_cached_package (510), pkg_cache snprintf (562), and registry_download_package (565), there is NO pkg_path_component_is_safe() call. The manifest path DOES validate at line 677 with an explicit anti-traversal comment (674-676), confirming the gap is the registry-supplied version. (2) registry_fetch_versions (356-370) extracts version strings verbatim via memchr/strndup with zero validation, so a body like {"versions":["../../../../../../../../tmp/pwned"]} yields versions[0] unchanged. (3) pkg_semver_satisfies line 151 returns true unconditionally for constraint "*", and "*" passes pkg_path_component_is_safe (no '/', no '..'), so `evil = "*"` is accepted at line 677. (4) find_best_version (379-389): with best==NULL the `!best` short-circuits the `||`, so pkg_semver_compare never runs and best=strdup(versions[0]) — the malicious string is returned. (5) fs_mkdir (src/fs_ops.c:100-112, POSIX branch) is a single mkdir(path,0755); the kernel resolves `..` lexically through real dirs. The name subdir (~/.lattice/packages/evil) is pre-created at lines 553-560, giving the climb a valid starting directory, and /tmp exists, so mkdir creates /tmp/pwned. (6) builtin_write_file (src/builtins.c:75-83) is a plain fopen(path,"w")+fputs(resp->body) with no sanitization, writing <dest_dir>/main.lat plus a generated lattice.toml. (7) Reachable: fetch_package (1313) -> fetch_from_registry (1387) is the HTTP fallback for plain version deps invoked by `clat install`. The URL `..` segments are irrelevant because the attacker controls the registry and returns 200 regardless; the traversal lives in the filesystem path pkg_cache, not the URL. Precondition is a malicious/MITM/attacker-set LATTICE_REGISTRY (default is HTTPS-pinned, HTTP needs LATTICE_INSECURE_REGISTRY) — which is exactly the threat model the code's own HTTPS enforcement and manifest validation already adopt. Note: the written filename is fixed to main.lat/lattice.toml, so it is an arbitrary-DIRECTORY write of attacker content (can clobber any existing dir's main.lat/lattice.toml, e.g. another lattice project's source, or create dirs anywhere user-writable), not a fully arbitrary filename — still a real containment escape beyond the intended cache directory. High is appropriate. || [high/high] Confirmed against src/package.c. The registry-returned version string is used as a filesystem path component with no sanitization. Chain verified: (1) registry_fetch_versions (lines 285-375) strndup's quoted JSON contents verbatim, no validation. (2) find_best_version (379-389): pkg_semver_satisfies("*", x) short-circuits to true at line 151 before any parse, and the `!best` branch at 383 selects versions[0] unchanged, so resolved = attacker string e.g. "../../../../../../../../tmp/pwned". (3) grep over lines 480-597 confirms resolved is NEVER passed through pkg_path_component_is_safe or any strstr(\"..\") check before being used in find_cached_package (line 510) and pkg_cache (line 562). This is exactly the check that IS applied to manifest versions at line 677 (pkg_path_component_is_safe(d->version)) — and note `*` itself passes that check (lines 85-93), so `evil = \"*\"` is accepted. (4) fs_mkdir (fs_ops.c:100-112) is a single mkdir(path,0755) with no `..` handling; the name subdir ~/.lattice/packages/evil is pre-created (lines 553-560) and parents by ensure_cache_dir, so the `..` climb resolves through existing dirs and clamps at `/`, creating /tmp/pwned, then builtin_write_file (builtins.c:75, raw fopen \"w\", no sanitization) writes the attacker-controlled HTTP body to <dir>/main.lat and a generated lattice.toml. Reachable via `clat install`: pkg_install loop (1535) -> fetch_package (1313) -> fetch_from_registry (1387). Threat model (compromised/MITM/attacker-controlled registry) is the one the code itself defends against elsewhere (HTTPS opt-out at 253-260, manifest validation at 677). The traversal grants arbitrary-directory file write outside lat_modules at install time (and can clobber main.lat/lattice.toml in any writable existing dir by targeting it directly, since fs_is_dir true skips mkdir) — a real escalation beyond a malicious registry merely serving package code. Could not refute. High severity given attacker-controlled-content arbitrary file write enabling e.g. overwriting shell rc files for code execution; precondition of an untrusted registry keeps it from critical.

confirmed.title: Env reference count is non-atomic while envs are shared across threads via closure capture (data race -> premature free / double-free)
confirmed.consequence: Concurrent non-atomic read-modify-write on env->refcount loses updates. A lost increment makes the count too low; a later env_release then reaches 0 while live closures (parent's f, the other child's detached closure) still reference E, so env_free runs and frees E->scopes (and the captured `n`) while another holder still points at it -> use-after-free and double-free of the scope hashmaps. The channel refcount uses __atomic precisely to avoid this; the env refcount does not. (Related: when the shared closure is actually invoked from two spawn threads, call_closure does ev->env=E + env_push_scope(E) which reallocs E->scopes and bumps E->count with no lock -> concurrent realloc/heap corruption.)
confirmed.verifier_notes: [high/high] VERIFIED REACHABLE. Every link confirmed against source. (1) Env.refcount is a plain non-atomic size_t (include/env.h:15) and env_retain/env_release (src/env.c:39-46) use bare ++/-- with no lock — unlike channel.c:19-22 which uses __atomic_* for exactly this cross-thread refcount, showing the project's own standard. (2) Tree-walk closures SHARE captured_env: value_clone_impl (src/value.c:519-522) takes `out.captured_env = v->captured_env; env_retain(...)` when body!=NULL and no arena is active; env_clone's malloc path (env.c:226-249) deep-clones each entry via value_deep_clone, so each child evaluator's cloned closure (f1, f2) holds the SAME Env* E as the parent's f. (3) create_child_evaluator (eval.c:1512-1522) clones the whole parent env (no filtering of closures) for every spawn; the example `let f = make(); scope { spawn{} spawn{} }` places f in scope[0], which is cloned into both children. (4) Each spawn thread, with NO synchronization, runs env_detach_values at exit (eval.c:1617) → env_detach_entry (env.c:61-68) does value_detach→env_retain(E) (value.c:46→521) then value_free→env_release(E) (value.c:1539-1540). Two sibling threads (pthread_create eval.c:9538, joined only at 9543) execute this concurrent non-atomic RMW on the same E->refcount. A lost increment leaves the count low; later sequential frees in free_child_evaluator (eval.c:1562 env_free→scope_free→value_free f1/f2→env_release E) then reach 0 while the parent's still-live f references E → env_free(E) frees E->scopes (and captured n) under a live holder = UAF + double-free. Reachability checks for a guard found none: --tree-walk is a real supported mode (main.c:1505), the scope/spawn handler (eval.c:9480-9564) is full native pthread concurrency (not stubbed, only WASM-excluded), and the sole mutex in the path (eval.c:10170) is select-specific. The race needs only f present in an enclosing scope, not f being invoked, so the primary trigger holds independent of the secondary call_closure realloc concern. confirmed.title: OP_BUILD_TUPLE leaks every popped element value (value_tuple deep-clones, originals never freed)
confirmed.trigger: Any program that constructs a tuple containing a non-primitive value, e.g. `let s = "...long..."; let t = (s, 1)`. Each construction leaks one heap copy. Verified: a 2,000,000-iteration loop building `(s, i)` peaks at 454 MB RSS vs 24 MB for the same loop without the tuple; halving iterations halves the leak (linear), confirming a per-construction leak.
confirmed.consequence: OP_BUILD_TUPLE pops `count` OWNED values into elems[], then value_tuple (src/value.c:308) DEEP-CLONES each element into the tuple. The popped originals in elems[] are never value_free'd (only the elems array itself is freed when count>16), so every non-primitive element's heap allocation (strings, arrays, structs, maps, nested tuples) is leaked on every tuple construction. value_array/value_struct_vm take ownership (memcpy/assign) — value_tuple's clone semantics make this opcode's ownership handling wrong. Unbounded memory growth → OOM/DoS in long-running or looping code.

confirmed.title: OP_BUILD_ENUM leaks every popped payload value (value_enum deep-clones, originals never freed)
confirmed.trigger: Any program that constructs an enum variant with a non-primitive payload, e.g. `enum Box{Wrap(any)} ... let e = Box::Wrap(s)` or `Box::Wrap([i,i+1,i+2])`. OP_BUILD_ENUM (src/stackvm.c:4632-4648) pops payload_count OWNED values into payload[] (line 4643), calls value_enum, then `free(payload)` (4646) which frees ONLY the temporary array container — there is no value_free of the individual LatValues, in this opcode or inside value_enum. value_enum (src/value.c:283-296) line 289 DEEP-CLONES each input: `val.as.enm.payload[i] = value_deep_clone(&payload[i])`, allocating fresh storage and never consuming the inputs. confirmed.title: Conditional/iter/handler jump offsets silently truncated to int16_t with no range check
confirmed.code: int16_t offset = (int16_t)(current_chunk()->code_len - instr_idx - 1);
confirmed.trigger: A function/if-else/match/try body large enough that a forward conditional jump (ROP_JMPFALSE/JMPTRUE/JMPNOTNIL/ITERNEXT/PUSH_HANDLER, all AsBx 16-bit) spans more than 32767 instructions (a few thousand lines of generated Lattice). patch_jump casts the distance to int16_t with no overflow detection.
confirmed.consequence: The 16-bit signed offset wraps to a wrong (possibly negative) value. The regvm dispatch loop has no ip-bounds check and trusts the offset, so control flow jumps to the wrong instruction (e.g. backwards, or into the middle of a logically-unrelated region), causing mis-execution, infinite loops, or crashes. Unlike emit_jmp_placeholder (24-bit), the conditional jumps cannot be widened, and there is no compile-time diagnostic — the bad code is emitted silently.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] Verified at src/regcompiler.c:193-200: patch_jump casts the forward distance to int16_t with no range check and re-encodes via REG_ENCODE_AsBx (regopcode.h:24), a 16-bit signed field (range -32768..32767, decoded as int16_t at regopcode.h:34). The regvm dispatch trusts it: regvm.c:3504-3516 does `frame->ip += offset` for JMPFALSE/JMPTRUE with no ip-bounds check. This contrasts with patch_jmp/REG_ENCODE_sBx, which uses a 24-bit field (sBx24, ±0x7FFFFF) for unconditional jumps — confirming the finder's widening asymmetry.

Searched upstream for any guard capping chunk instruction count below 32768 and found NONE: (1) regchunk_write (regvm.c:111-124) grows code unboundedly via realloc; (2) REGVM_CONST_MAX (65536) does not bound instruction count because regchunk_add_constant (regvm.c:126-140) deduplicates both string and int constants, and most emitted instructions (MOVE/ADD/jumps/local access) add no constants — e.g. thousands of `x = x + 1` reuse one constant while emitting 30k+ instructions; (3) REGVM_REG_MAX (256) limits registers, not instructions (registers are recycled via free_regs_to between statements); (4) no validation pass or diagnostic exists for oversized conditional jumps (grep found nothing).

Concrete reachable trigger: `if cond { <then-branch with >32767 instructions> } else {...}` — the ROP_JMPFALSE emitted at regcompiler.c:701 and patched at 719 spans the entire then-branch; a body exceeding 32767 instructions (~10k lines of generated Lattice, achievable by codegen like latc.lat/anneal) silently truncates the offset to a wrong/negative value, corrupting control flow. The regvm is a shipped backend reachable via `clat --regvm file.lat` and `compile --regvm` (Makefile:386,500). Genuinely reachable silent miscompilation with no compile-time diagnostic; medium severity is appropriate since it requires a pathologically large single function body but produces silent wrong code / crashes when hit. || [high/medium] Mechanism verified against the actual code. src/regcompiler.c:195 computes `int16_t offset = (int16_t)(current_chunk()->code_len - instr_idx - 1)` and stores it via REG_ENCODE_AsBx with NO range check. The AsBx sBx field is genuinely 16-bit signed (include/regopcode.h:23-24 encode `(uint16_t)(int16_t)(sbx)`; REG_GET_sBx decodes int16_t; range -32768..32767). patch_jump (28 call sites) services exactly the conditional jumps named: if/else else_jump (719), match arm/fail jumps, JMPNOTNIL (1441), PUSH_HANDLER (1278/2272/2384), and loop/iter exits. The regvm dispatch decodes these with REG_GET_sBx and does `frame->ip += offset` with no ip-bounds check (regvm.c:3506-3514, 4867-4868, 4876-4879). The asymmetry is real: patch_jmp (line 203) uses int32_t into a 24-bit biased field (±~8M) so unconditional jumps don't wrap at the same point; the conditional jumps cannot be widened. No compile-time guard exists for the offset, unlike the sibling guards add_constant (errors at REGVM_CONST_MAX=65535, line 231) and alloc_reg (register overflow, line 147); regchunk_write just grows, so code_len is uncapped. The bounds-checking verifier (regchunk_verify, RCK_JUMP at regvm.c:7367) runs ONLY for loaded .rlatc bytecode in latc.c:1002 — the normal compile-and-run path (main.c:290/471 -> regvm_run) skips it, so a freshly compiled truncated jump executes directly; and even verified, a wrapped offset landing on a valid-but-wrong instruction start passes RCK_JUMP. Trigger requires a single jumped-over region exceeding 32767 register instructions — large but reachable for generated/large code and unbounded by any limit. When hit, the wrapped (likely negative) offset causes backward/out-of-bounds jumps -> infinite loops, mis-execution, or OOB instruction fetch, emitted silently with no diagnostic. The finder did not misread types, bounds, ownership, or control flow. Medium severity is appropriate: serious silent miscompile/possible OOB but gated behind an unusually large contiguous region. || [high/medium] Verified against source. patch_jump (src/regcompiler.c:195) does `int16_t offset = (int16_t)(current_chunk()->code_len - instr_idx - 1);` with NO range check and NO rc_error on overflow. The AsBx slot is genuinely 16-bit: REG_ENCODE_AsBx (include/regopcode.h:23) masks through (uint16_t)(int16_t), and the VM decodes with REG_GET_sBx -> int16_t, range +/-32767. Medium severity stands: silent miscompilation plus potential OOB instruction fetch/crash.

confirmed.title: Unbounded recursion in value_free / value_clone_impl (and the whole recursive value-op family) → stack-overflow crash on deeply nested data
confirmed.trigger:
A Lattice program that builds a deeply nested value at runtime, e.g.

    flux a = [0]
    for i in 0..200000 { a = [a] }

Arrays/structs/tuples/enums are value types whose elements are themselves LatValues, so each iteration nests one level deeper. Every recursive value operation (value_clone_impl when [a] clones the current array, value_free when the previous binding is released, plus value_eq, value_display, value_hash_key, set_phase_recursive, region_tag_recursive, value_is_shareable, find_unsendable_msg) descends one C stack frame per nesting level with no depth cap. At ~50k-80k levels the default 8MB stack is exhausted.
confirmed.consequence: Stack overflow → SIGSEGV (denial of service). The crash is unavoidable: even normal cleanup of the binding at scope exit calls value_free, which recurses to the full nesting depth. There is no depth guard anywhere in value.c, so any of these value-core entry points (clone, free, equality used by `==`, display/print, freeze/thaw phase walk, channel-send eligibility scan) crashes the interpreter on adversarially (or accidentally) deep structures. Reachable from pure Lattice source with no FFI or malformed input required.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] VERIFIED REACHABLE AND REPRODUCED. The cited snippet matches src/value.c:1515-1517 exactly. value_free (src/value.c:1498) recurses per array element (line 1516) with no depth guard, and value_clone_impl (src/value.c:444, VAL_ARRAY case line 471) recurses per element with no depth guard; set_phase_recursive (709) and region_tag_recursive (885) are likewise uncapped. No upstream validator/compiler invariant bounds runtime nesting depth — the deep nesting is built dynamically by the loop body `a = [a]` (arrays are value types whose elems are LatValues), so no source/parser nesting limit applies.

Empirical confirmation (built clat from the working tree, default bytecode VM backend, no --tree-walk):
- Exact report trigger (`flux a=[0]; for i in 0..200000 { a=[a] }`) on the default 8MB stack -> SIGSEGV (exit 139), "done" never printed.
- Control: 1000-deep nesting on 8MB stack completes cleanly (exit 0); the SAME 1000-deep nesting under `ulimit -s 256` -> SIGSEGV (139). The crash threshold scales with both nesting depth and stack size — the unambiguous signature of unbounded C recursion exhausting the stack, not an unrelated fault.

Reachable from pure Lattice source on the default execution path, no FFI / no malformed file. Severity: it is a clean stack-overflow SIGSEGV (guard-page abort), i.e. a denial-of-service / availability crash of the whole interpreter process, NOT memory corruption or RCE. It requires the program to construct a pathologically deep (~50k+) structure, so accidental triggering is uncommon, but it is a realistic DoS vector in embedded/untrusted-script or web-framework (anneal) contexts and the entire host process dies. Medium is defensible (matches finder) given default-backend reachability and full-process crash; one could argue low given no corruption/escalation. confirmed.title: gc_mark_value recurses without a cycle/visited guard → stack-overflow SIGSEGV on reference cycles
confirmed.trigger:
Run `clat --gc-stress prog.lat` where prog.lat builds a reference cycle:

  flux r = Ref::new(0)
  r.set([r])
  let a = 1
  let b = 2

Ref::new + .set([r]) makes r->value an array containing a retained handle to r itself (value_detach/value_clone treat a Ref as a shared leaf and stop at the boundary, so the cycle is fully constructible and normal clone/free/print do NOT recurse into it). At each OP_RESET_EPHEMERAL safe point, gc_maybe_collect→gc_collect→gc_mark_roots marks r as a root and gc_mark_value follows VAL_REF→array→VAL_REF→... forever. gc_mark_value has only the `region_id != REGION_NONE` early-out (gc.c:129); it never checks whether the container's GCObject is already marked before recursing, so cyclic VAL_REF/VAL_ARRAY/VAL_STRUCT graphs recurse unbounded. The incremental path has the same defect via gc_trace_one/gc_gray_push_value (no already-marked check, gc.c:301-332,384-451): with `--gc-stress --gc-incremental` the gray stack grows without bound until realloc(gc.c:329, unchecked) returns NULL and `gray_stack[gray_count++]=val` null-derefs.

The reference cycle is a fully supported language construct (value.h documents `r.set([r])`); only the GC root traversal is unsafe for it.

confirmed.title: 32-bit unsigned overflow in malloc(slen+1)/malloc(nlen+1) for length-prefixed strings -> heap buffer overflow
confirmed.trigger: A .latc whose string-constant length field slen == 0xFFFFFFFF. slen is uint32_t, so `slen + 1` is computed in unsigned 32-bit arithmetic and wraps to 0 -> malloc(0) returns a minimal (non-NULL on macOS/glibc) allocation. br_read_bytes() then does memcpy(s, data+pos, 0xFFFFFFFF) whenever pos+0xFFFFFFFF <= len, i.e. when the input file is ~4GB. Same un-cast pattern at lines 426 (local name), 450 (chunk name), 852 and 938 (regvm const string / local name). Note line 520 uses the CORRECT form `malloc((size_t)slen + 1)`, proving the omission is a bug, not intentional.
confirmed.consequence: memcpy of up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes (fully attacker-controlled content) into a 0/16-byte heap allocation -> heap buffer overflow / memory corruption (then s[slen]='\0' writes far OOB). On 32-bit/wasm32 builds size_t is 32-bit so the same wrap plus undersized allocations are far easier to hit. Caveat: on 64-bit the wrap point 0xFFFFFFFF requires a ~4GB input for br_read_bytes to actually perform the copy.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] REACHABILITY CONFIRMED — no upstream guard prevents the trigger. Path: `clat file.latc` -> main.c run_latc_file (src/main.c:451/488) -> chunk_load/regchunk_load (latc.c:630/1028) -> chunk_deserialize/regchunk_deserialize -> deserialize_chunk(br, err, 0). chunk_load does `malloc(len); fread(data,1,len,f)` with NO file-size cap. The only header validation before the vulnerable code is magic (4 bytes "LATC"/"RLAT") + version u16 + reserved u16 — no checksum, no slen bound. Between `br_read_u32_le(br,&slen)` (line 343) and `malloc(slen+1)` (line 348) there is zero validation of slen. The recursion `depth>200` bound (line 247) only limits closure nesting, not string length. chunk_verify runs AFTER deserialize_chunk returns, so it cannot prevent corruption that happens during parsing.

The overflow is real: `slen+1` with slen=0xFFFFFFFF computes in unsigned 32-bit -> 0 -> malloc(0). I confirmed line 520 uses the correct `malloc((size_t)slen + 1)` form, so the missing cast at 348/426/450/852/938 is a genuine bug, not intentional.

PLATFORM NUANCE (refines, does not refute): br_read_bytes (line 120) checks `if (br->pos + n > br->len) return false;` in size_t arithmetic, and slen is passed as size_t n.
- On 64-bit (the user's primary macOS build): n = (size_t)0xFFFFFFFF (~4GB), pos+n does NOT wrap, so the memcpy only proceeds if the input file is >= ~4GB. With a normal-sized file br_read_bytes returns false, frees s, and `s[slen]='\0'` (line 356) is never reached -> the malloc(0) is benign. So practical heap overflow on 64-bit requires a ~4GB attacker file (feasible but heavy).
- On 32-bit / wasm32 (`make wasm` is a documented target): size_t is 32-bit, so `pos + 0xFFFFFFFF` wraps to pos-1, the bounds check passes for a SMALL file, and memcpy of 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into a malloc(0) buffer triggers trivially -> easy heap overflow / OOB write of `s[slen]`.

The regvm path (line 852) additionally omits the `if (!s) return NULL` NULL-check entirely. Verdict: genuinely reachable from malformed untrusted .latc/.rlatc input with no preventing guard; a real latent integer-overflow in a security-sensitive deserializer. Keeping medium: trivially exploitable on 32-bit/wasm, gated by a ~4GB-file precondition on the default 64-bit build. || [high/medium] Verified directly in src/latc.c. When that holds, memcpy copies ~4GB of attacker-controlled bytes into the 0-byte allocation and `s[slen]='\0'` writes far OOB -> heap buffer overflow. The finder's contrast with line 520, which correctly uses `malloc((size_t)slen + 1)` (forces 64-bit, request becomes 4GB and fails to NULL -> no overflow), confirms the missing cast is the bug, not intentional. Real memory-safety bug. Keeping medium: it is an attacker-controlled-file heap overflow (normally high), but on the default 64-bit build it requires a ~4.3GB malicious file, a substantial practical barrier that justifies medium rather than high; only the wasm32/32-bit path is easy. || [high/medium] VERIFIED against src/latc.c, not refuted. Reachability is confirmed: chunk_load/regchunk_load deserialize attacker-supplied .latc/.rlatc files (running `clat file.latc` per project memory), and chunk_verify runs only after deserialize_chunk returns, so it cannot prevent the in-loop memcpy. I found no clone, bound, or cap that makes it safe. Severity kept at medium: it is a genuine attacker-controlled heap buffer overflow, but on the primary 64-bit platform the ~4GB-input requirement is a substantial practical barrier (only 32-bit/wasm builds make it trivially triggerable), and the threat model already presupposes loading untrusted bytecode.

confirmed.title: Attacker-controlled line_count used as allocation size with unchecked realloc result (and 32-bit multiply overflow) in line-table deserialization
confirmed.trigger: A ~21-byte .latc with the line-table count field set to a huge value (e.g. line_count = 0xFFFFFFFF) followed by >=4 bytes of line data. line_count is read straight from the file (br_read_u32_le) and is NOT bounded by the number of remaining input bytes, yet it is used immediately as the realloc element count BEFORE any line data is read. The realloc return value is never checked for NULL before the loop writes c->lines[i]. Verified with a PoC that reaches the write loop: a 21-byte file requests a 16GB realloc. Identical pattern in the regvm path at lines 785-797.
confirmed.consequence: When the 16GB realloc fails (32-bit/wasm32 builds, or 64-bit under RLIMIT_AS / address-space-limited sandboxes), c->lines becomes NULL and `c->lines[i] = ...` dereferences NULL -> crash/DoS while merely loading a .latc. On 32-bit builds `c->lines_cap * sizeof(int)` (e.g. line_count=0x40000001 -> *4 truncates to 4) yields an undersized buffer that the read loop then overflows -> heap buffer overflow. On unconstrained 64-bit hosts the 16GB reservation succeeds lazily and degrades to a benign truncated-read error, which is why the existing 64KB-capped fuzz harness never catches it.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] The cited code is verified verbatim at src/latc.c:281-293 (stack VM) and the identical pattern at src/latc.c:785-797 (regvm). The path is genuinely reachable from attacker-controlled input.

- CLI entry: `clat file.latc` -> src/main.c:435-488 reads the 4-byte magic, dispatches RLATC->regchunk_load (main.c:451) or LATC->chunk_load (main.c:488). Also reachable via runtime.c:3767 and runtime_main.c. The codebase comments themselves call these files "untrusted .latc/.rlatc" (latc.c:245, 749) and run chunk_verify on the result (latc.c:601-609), so adversarial .latc input is the explicit threat model.
- chunk_deserialize (latc.c:566) only validates len>=8, 4-byte magic (LATC_MAGIC), the u16 version, and a u16 reserved field, then calls deserialize_chunk. NO upstream validator bounds line_count.
- Inside deserialize_chunk: code_len is read; the code realloc IS effectively protected because br_read_bytes (latc.c:120-125) bounds-checks against br->len so a huge code_len fails cleanly. But line_count (latc.c:275-280) is read raw via br_read_u32_le with NO bound against remaining input, and is used IMMEDIATELY at lines 281-283 as the realloc element count BEFORE any line data is read. chunk_verify (latc.c:603) runs only AFTER deserialize_chunk returns, so it cannot guard this loop.

NO GUARD EXISTS: There is no min(line_count, remaining_bytes) check, no per-chunk size sanity cap, and the realloc return is never checked for NULL before `c->lines[i] = ...` (line 292 / 796). A ~20-byte file (magic+version+reserved + code_len=0 + line_count=0xFFFFFFFF + one u32 line value) reaches the write loop and requests realloc(NULL/old, 0xFFFFFFFF*4 ~= 16GB).

confirmed.title: Unbounded recursion in parse_pattern → C stack overflow (DoS)
confirmed.trigger: A match expression with a deeply nested pattern, e.g. a file containing: `match 0 {` followed by ~60000 '[' characters. Each '[' makes parse_pattern recurse into itself.

Deeply nested patterns overflow the C stack → SIGSEGV. Confirmed under ASan: 'stack-overflow' with parse_pattern recursing into itself. Untrusted source crashes the process. The depth guard that the codebase explicitly added (see comment at parser.c:19-21) is meant to prevent exactly this but was never applied to pattern parsing.

confirmed.title: Unbounded recursion in YAML nested-mapping value parsing bypasses YAML_MAX_DEPTH (stack overflow)
confirmed.code:
