confirmed.title: Set.clear() leaks all stored element values (lat_map_free does not free inline values)
confirmed.trigger: flux s = Set::new(); s.add("leakme"); s.clear();  // each cleared string/array/struct element's deep-clone is leaked; wrap in a loop (loop { s.add(big); s.clear(); }) for unbounded growth / DoS
confirmed.consequence: Set elements are stored as heap-owning value_deep_clone() copies (builtin_set_add, line 1449). lat_map_free() only frees keys + the entries array ('value is inline — no free needed' in ds/hashmap.c) and the dispatcher (stackvm.c:2340-2342) does not free values either. value_free()'s VAL_SET case freed each value first precisely because lat_map_free does not — clear() bypasses that, permanently leaking every element's heap memory (str_val, array.elems, nested maps, etc.) under the default malloc allocator.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] Verified the full chain in the actual source; the path is genuinely reachable from ordinary Lattice code in the default backend.

MECHANISM CONFIRMED:
- builtin_set_clear (src/builtin_methods.c:1591-1598) calls lat_map_free(obj->as.set.map) then re-inits with lat_map_new — exactly as quoted.
- lat_map_free (src/ds/hashmap.c:38-50) frees ONLY entry->key and the entries array; comment line 42 literally says "value is inline — no free needed". It never inspects the stored LatValue, so any heap the value owns (str_val, array.elems, nested map, struct, etc.) is orphaned.
- Set elements are heap-owning deep clones: builtin_set_add (line 1449) does value_deep_clone(&args[0]) and lat_map_set copies that LatValue into the entry's inline _ibuf, transferring ownership of str_val to the map. value_deep_clone of a string uses lat_strdup (value.c:463); strings are documented heap-allocated (value.h:60 "char *str_val; /* heap-allocated string */"), no SSO.
- The correct/reference free path proves the contract: value_free's VAL_SET case (value.c:1570-1581) explicitly loops every occupied entry calling value_free(sv) BEFORE lat_map_free, precisely because lat_map_free does not. clear() skips that loop, so every element's heap leaks.

REACHABILITY (my focus): No upstream guard prevents the trigger. clear() on a set dispatches to builtin_set_clear in ALL backends with no element-freeing in the dispatcher: default bytecode VM stackvm.c:2340-2343 (push(vm, builtin_set_clear(obj, NULL, 0, &err))), regvm.c:2101-2103, and tree-walker eval.c:7985-7994 (which inlines the identical leaky lat_map_free+lat_map_new). obj is a borrowed pointer to the live set (e.g. &frame->slots[slot] / env_get_ref), so s.add(...) populates it and s.clear() frees it in place. Ran the trigger (flux s = Set::new(); s.add("leakme"); s.clear()) under ./clat_asan — executes cleanly (exit 0), confirming the path is live (macOS LSan unavailable, so no leak dump, but the static chain is unambiguous).

ARENA CHECK: g_arena (value.c:20-28) is NULL during normal execution — only set transiently during arena-clone. With g_arena==NULL, lat_strdup == real strdup (heap), so under the default malloc allocator the cleared clones are true leaks, exactly as the finding scopes it.

SEVERITY: Bounded per-call leak proportional to element heap; in a loop { s.add(big); s.clear() } it is unbounded growth → memory-exhaustion DoS. No corruption/UAF/RCE. Medium is correct. || [high/medium] Mechanism re-derived from source and confirmed. builtin_set_add (src/builtin_methods.c:1449-1450) inserts value_deep_clone() copies into the set's map; value_clone_impl heap-allocates owned memory for non-interned strings (value.c:463 lat_strdup), arrays (value.c:469 lat_alloc), structs, nested maps, etc. lat_map_free (src/ds/hashmap.c:38-50) frees only entry->key and the entries array — the value lives inline in LatMapEntry._ibuf (hashmap.h:20-25) and the code explicitly states '/* value is inline — no free needed */' (line 42); it never calls value_free, so the heap memory referenced by each inline LatValue is leaked. The proof that callers must free values themselves is value_free's VAL_SET case (src/value.c:1570-1581), which loops over every occupied entry calling value_free(sv) BEFORE lat_map_free — precisely because lat_map_free does not. builtin_set_clear (src/builtin_methods.c:1591-1598) calls lat_map_free directly with no preceding per-value free, then re-news the map, leaking every element's owned heap memory. confirmed.title: Map.remove() leaks the removed value (lat_map_remove does not free inline value)
confirmed.trigger: flux m = Map::new(); m["k"] = "leakme"; m.remove("k");  // the stored value clone is leaked; loop { m["k"]=big; m.remove("k"); } leaks unboundedly
confirmed.consequence: The map owns heap value clones (value_free's VAL_MAP case frees each stored value before lat_map_free). lat_map_remove() only frees the entry key and tombstones the slot ('value is inline, no free needed' in ds/hashmap.c); the dispatcher (stackvm.c:2019-2026) frees only the key argument, never the stored value. Removing any string/array/map/struct value permanently leaks its heap memory — unbounded leak / DoS over a program's lifetime.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] VERIFIED REAL and reachable from ordinary user code.

Code path (verified against the files):
1. src/builtin_methods.c:1103-1108 — builtin_map_remove() calls lat_map_remove(obj->as.map.map, key) for a VAL_STR key and returns value_unit(). It never frees the stored value.
2. src/ds/hashmap.c:145-162 — lat_map_remove() free()s only e->key, sets e->state = MAP_TOMBSTONE, with the comment "value is inline, no free needed". The value is a LatValue stored inline in e->_ibuf; for VAL_STR/array/map/struct that LatValue owns heap memory (e.g. as.str_val) that is NOT reclaimed.
3. The map owns heap clones: src/value.c value_free() VAL_MAP case iterates entries and value_free()s each LatValue, but ONLY for state==MAP_OCCUPIED. Tombstoned slots are skipped, so a removed entry's heap memory is never freed at map teardown either.
4. The stored value is genuinely heap-owned (REGION_NONE), not arena memory: OP_SET_INDEX_LOCAL (stackvm.c:6263) and OP_SET_INDEX_GLOBAL (stackvm.c:6585) both call stackvm_promote_value(&val) before lat_map_set(), promoting ephemeral-arena values to malloc'd clones. value_free does free VAL_STR via val_dealloc (value.c:1514).
5. Dispatcher (src/stackvm.c:2019-2026) frees only args[0] (the key) via value_free(&args[0]) and pushes the unit result; it never touches the stored value. Same defect in regvm.c:2134 and eval.c:7936 (all three backends call the same lat_map_remove).

Reachability — no upstream guard blocks the trigger. The only relevant guard is the LAT-441 phase check (eval.c:7633+ / phase_check_index_write), which rejects mutating methods only on CRYSTAL/SUBLIMATED receivers. A flux Map (the trigger's `flux m = Map::new()`) is mutable, so remove proceeds. `map.remove(key)` is a normal, documented method.

Empirical confirmation (existing clat_asan, macOS lacks LSan so used RSS scaling): the loop `m["k"]=<88-char string>; m.remove("k")` peaks at ~26 MB for 20k iterations vs ~115 MB for 2M iterations — unbounded growth proportional to iteration count. Negative control doing the identical string allocation into a reassigned local (which frees the old) for 2M iterations stays flat at ~25 MB, proving the growth is the leaked map values, not the allocation harness.

Severity medium is appropriate: a deterministic, user-triggerable unbounded heap leak (string/array/map/struct/set values), leading to memory exhaustion/DoS in long-running programs (e.g. the map-heavy anneal web framework), but not memory corruption or an immediate crash. || [high/medium] Verified the full ownership chain against the actual code; the claim holds.

OWNERSHIP — the map owns deep-cloned, heap-resident LatValues:
- Lattice Maps are created with `lat_map_new(sizeof(LatValue))` (value.c:271). The hashmap stores the full LatValue *inline* in the 72-byte `_ibuf` (hashmap.h:17-25); `value` always points at `_ibuf`.
- The clone path (value.c:554-589, value_clone_impl VAL_MAP) deep-clones each stored value into the new map via `value_clone_impl(sv)`, so a stored VAL_STR carries its own malloc'd `str_val`.
- The set path (stackvm.c OP_SET_INDEX, 4853-4855) does `lat_map_set(obj.as.map.map, idx.as.str_val, &val)` and frees only the key (`idx`), NOT `val` — i.e. ownership of `val`'s heap is moved into the map.
- value_free's VAL_MAP case (value.c:1542-1558) iterates occupied entries and calls `value_free(mv)` on each stored LatValue before `lat_map_free`. This proves the map owns each stored value's heap.

THE LEAK — lat_map_remove drops the value without freeing it:
- lat_map_remove (ds/hashmap.c:145-162) frees only `e->key`, sets `e->state = MAP_TOMBSTONE`, and explicitly comments "value is inline, no free needed" — it never frees the inline LatValue's heap members. Because the slot becomes a TOMBSTONE, the later map-teardown loop (which only processes MAP_OCCUPIED) skips it, so the heap is permanently unreachable.
- builtin_map_remove (builtin_methods.c:1103-1108) calls lat_map_remove directly, no prior lat_map_get + value_free.
- The bytecode dispatcher (stackvm.c:2019-2026) frees only `args[0]` (the key string), never the stored value. The regvm path (regvm.c:2130-2137) calls lat_map_remove inline and likewise never frees the stored value.

CONFIRMING REFERENCE: the tree-walker does it correctly (eval.c:7933-7937): `LatValue *old = lat_map_get(...); if (old) value_free(old); lat_map_remove(...);`. The two VM backends omit exactly this `value_free(old)` step — a clear inconsistency that confirms the omission is a bug, not intended borrow semantics.

TRIGGER: `flux m = Map::new(); m["k"]="leakme"; m.remove("k");` leaks the string's heap. `loop { m["k"]=big; m.remove("k"); }` leaks one `big` per iteration (after remove tombstones the slot, the next set reuses it as a fresh OCCUPIED entry), giving an unbounded leak.

Severity medium is appropriate: a genuine unbounded heap leak (long-running DoS) but not memory corruption / not a crash, and it requires the program to actively remove map entries. Confidence high. Note: this only leaks for heap-owned values (region_id == -1); arena/shared-region values are no-op-freed anyway, but the common string/array/map/struct case is heap-owned and leaks. A related but distinct leak exists at OP_SET_INDEX overwrite (it doesn't free the old value before lat_map_set), outside this finding's scope. || [high/medium] CONFIRMED real and triggerable on the default (stack VM) backend. I verified the mechanism in code and empirically reproduced the unbounded leak.

Code path (all paths absolute):
- src/builtin_methods.c:1103-1108 — builtin_map_remove() calls lat_map_remove() and returns value_unit(); it never fetches/frees the stored value.
- src/stackvm.c:2019-2026 — dispatcher value_free()s only args[0] (the key), then pushes unit. The stored value is never freed.
- src/ds/hashmap.c:145-162 — lat_map_remove() only free()s e->key and sets e->state = MAP_TOMBSTONE ("value is inline, no free needed"). The inline _ibuf still holds the LatValue (e.g. VAL_STR with a heap str_val pointer); that heap memory is never released.
- src/value.c:1542-1551 — value_free's VAL_MAP case proves the map OWNS heap clones: it iterates only MAP_OCCUPIED entries and value_free()s each stored LatValue before lat_map_free(). Tombstoned slots are skipped, so the removed value is unreachable and leaked even at full map teardown.
- src/ds/hashmap.c:117/124 lat_map_get returns e->value (pointer into inline buf), and SET_INDEX (src/stackvm.c:6319) transfers ownership of val into the map without freeing — so the stored value is a genuine REGION_NONE heap allocation owned by the map.

Decisive corroboration: the tree-walker does it CORRECTLY at src/eval.c:7933-7936 — it does `LatValue *old = lat_map_get(...); if (old) value_free(old);` BEFORE lat_map_remove(). This establishes the intended contract (caller must free the stored value first) and shows the stack-VM and regvm paths omit it. The regvm path src/regvm.c:2134 has the identical bug.

Empirical proof (LeakSanitizer is unsupported on macOS, so I measured max RSS scaling with a freshly built ASan binary of the current tree): a loop that, per iteration, inserts a DISTINCT key with a 100KB string then removes it (map ends size=0) scaled linearly — 2000 iters = 284MB, 20000 iters = 2.62GB — matching ~20000×100KB leaked. A control loop allocating/freeing the same local strings stayed flat (284MB→372MB). This isolates the leak to remove (inserts hit the new-entry branch, never the overwrite branch).

Consequence: every Map.remove()/delete() of a heap-backed value (string/array/map/struct/set/tuple/enum) permanently leaks that value on the two default-used backends. In a long-running server (e.g. Anneal sessions/caches that add+remove keys) this is an unbounded leak → eventual OOM/DoS. It is a pure leak (no UAF/corruption), so medium severity is appropriate. Finder's location, mechanism, and trigger are all accurate.

confirmed.title: Buffer.write_u64/write_i64/write_u8/u16/u32 read args[1].as.int_val without type check, leaking host heap/code pointers
confirmed.code:
size_t i = (size_t)args[0].as.int_val;
    uint64_t v = (uint64_t)args[1].as.int_val;
confirmed.trigger: flux b = <Buffer of length >= 8>; b.write_u64(0, "x"); let p = b.read_u64(0);  // p is the heap address of the string "x". Passing an array/closure leaks its elems/chunk pointer instead.
confirmed.consequence: All Buffer write_* methods validate the index (args[0]) is a VAL_INT but never validate the value (args[1]); they read args[1].as.int_val directly. The LatValue union overlaps int_val with str_val/array.elems/closure pointers, so passing a non-Int (e.g. a String) writes the raw 8-byte host pointer into the buffer. Reading it back (read_u64) discloses a host heap (or code) pointer to untrusted script code, defeating ASLR and providing an exploitation primitive. confirmed.title: parse_response reads uninitialized/out-of-bounds heap when server closes with no data
confirmed.trigger:
HTTP(S) request to a server that accepts the connection and immediately closes it without sending any bytes. In http_execute (src/http.c:346-391) the first net_tcp_read/net_tls_read returns an empty string (clen==0), so the loop breaks at line 367 with resp_len==0 while resp_buf (malloc'd at line 343) was never written or NUL-terminated. parse_response is then called at line 399 with an uninitialized buffer, and strstr(raw, "

") / strstr(raw, "
") scan uninitialized heap.
confirmed.consequence: Read of uninitialized heap memory; because the 8192-byte buffer has no guaranteed NUL terminator, strstr can scan past the end of the allocation (out-of-bounds read), potentially crashing the process (DoS) or, if a stale pattern is matched, mis-parsing arbitrary heap contents into the response. Triggerable remotely by any server the program connects to.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] Verified reachable end-to-end. User-facing builtins http_get/http_post/http_request are registered in runtime.c:4333-4335 and eval.c:4066+ (and the http module, runtime.c:3991-3993), all calling http_execute(&req,&err). In http_execute (src/http.c:340-401) resp_buf is malloc(8192) at line 343 with no memset and no NUL write. The claimed trigger hinges on what net_tcp_read/net_tls_read return on a server that closes with no data: I confirmed net_tcp_read (src/net.c:202-210) does recv, and since EOF gives n==0 (not <0) it executes buf[0]='\0'; return buf — a NON-NULL empty string. net_tls_read (OpenSSL, src/tls.c:114-122) behaves identically (SSL_read returns 0, n not <0, returns non-NULL ""), and the schannel path returns malloc'd "" on EOF too. Therefore the read loop bypasses the `if(!chunk)` guard at http.c:353 and instead hits the clen==0 branch at lines 367-370 (`free(chunk); break;`), leaving resp_len==0 and resp_buf fully uninitialized/un-terminated (the only NUL write, line 389, requires a prior non-empty memcpy). parse_response(resp_buf, 0, err) is then called unconditionally at line 399 — no resp_len guard — and line 139 strstr(raw,"

") scans uninitialized heap with no in-bounds NUL guarantee. No upstream validator, type check, or compiler invariant prevents this; the socket timeout doesn't apply because recv returns 0 immediately on a clean close. The function is compiled for all native (non-EMSCRIPTEN) builds. Most common runtime outcome is a graceful 'no header/body separator' error (heap often contains an early NUL), but on a recycled glibc arena block (8192 < mmap threshold) strstr can read past the allocation -> OOB read, crash/DoS, or rare misparse of stale heap. Remote-controllable by any server the program connects to. Medium is appropriate. || [high/medium] Mechanism verified against the actual source. At src/http.c:343 resp_buf is allocated with malloc (uninitialized, no NUL terminator), resp_len=0. The read loop's first call returns the connection's data: on a clean EOF (server accepts then immediately closes), recv returns 0 in net_tcp_read (src/net.c:202-210), giving buf[0]='\0' and a NON-NULL empty string; OpenSSL net_tls_read (src/tls.c:114-122) and Schannel net_tls_read (src/tls.c:463-468) likewise return non-NULL empty strings on EOF. Therefore the `if (!chunk)` error branch (http.c:353, which would return NULL when resp_len==0) is NOT taken; instead clen==strlen("")==0 hits the `free(chunk); break;` at http.c:367-370. The only place resp_buf is NUL-terminated (http.c:389) is reached only after memcpy of a non-empty chunk, so with an immediate empty read it never runs. parse_response is then called unconditionally at http.c:399 (no resp_len>0 guard) on the uninitialized buffer, and strstr(raw, "

") at http.c:139 scans uninitialized heap with no guaranteed NUL within the 8192-byte allocation -> potential out-of-bounds read past the allocation (DoS), or, if stale heap contains the separator pattern, mis-parsing of arbitrary heap memory into the response. The finder did not misread clone/borrow, inclusive/exclusive bounds, union members, or control flow; the critical empty-string-vs-NULL distinction is exactly what makes the buggy path reachable, and it holds across all three read backends. Remotely triggerable by any server the client connects to. Read-only undefined behavior (no write primitive), so medium severity is appropriate. || [high/low] Verified against the actual source; the finding is real. resp_buf is allocated uninitialized at src/http.c:343 (malloc, never calloc/memset). confirmed.title: channel_add_waiter inserts one waiter node into multiple channel lists via a single `next` pointer (list corruption / lost wakeups / select hang)
confirmed.trigger: Two concurrent `select` statements that share a channel, e.g. pass one Channel into two spawned tasks that each `select { recv(shared) => {}  recv(private) => {} }` while a third task sends on `shared`. Each select registers its single stack waiter on EVERY channel arm (src/eval.c:10234; same pattern in src/stackvm.c:8238, src/regvm.c:6100). Because LatSelectWaiter has only one `next`, a node cannot be a member of two channel lists at once. Interleaving: S1 add(shared,w1) makes shared=[w1],w1->next=NULL; S2 add(shared,w2) makes shared=[w2->w1]; then S2 add(private2,w2) executes `w2->next = private2->waiters` (=NULL), OVERWRITING w2->next which was w1. Now shared->waiters=w2, w2->next=NULL — w1 has been silently dropped from `shared`'s waiter list.
confirmed.consequence: A sender on the shared channel iterates ch->waiters and signals only the surviving node (w2), never w1, so select S1 stays blocked in pthread_cond_wait forever even though data is available -> lost wakeup / deadlock (the enclosing scope's pthread_join never returns, hanging the program). The cross-linking can also splice one channel's list into another, causing a send/close to traverse waiters that are not actually registered on that channel.
confirmed.verifier_notes: [high/medium] VERIFIED REACHABLE — no upstream guard. The cited code at src/channel.c:139-149 is exactly as claimed: channel_add_waiter does `w->next = ch->waiters; ch->waiters = w;`, and LatSelectWaiter (include/channel.h:15-21) has a single `next` pointer, so one node cannot belong to two lists at once. The select implementations register ONE stack waiter on EVERY channel arm via the same `&waiter`: src/eval.c:10172-10176 + 10234 (`for k: channel_add_waiter(channels[indices[k]], &waiter)`), mirrored in src/stackvm.c:8144/8238 and src/regvm.c:6001/6100. There is no global select lock; waiters are added only under each channel's own per-channel mutex, so two selects on different threads interleave freely. channel_send (src/channel.c:59-63) and channel_close (106-110) walk `ch->waiters` via `next` and signal only the reachable nodes. Concrete trigger trace: T1 registers w1 on shared (shared=[w1], w1.next=NULL after its own add to private1); T2 does add(shared,w2) -> shared=[w2->w1], then add(private2,w2) sets w2.next=private2->waiters=NULL, silently orphaning w1 from shared's list. A single send(shared) signals only w2; T1 is never woken and blocks forever in pthread_cond_wait (plain select, no timeout arm), so its spawning scope's join never returns and the program hangs. This is a genuine concurrency data-structure invariant violation reachable from ordinary, documented Lattice usage (spawn + shared channel + multi-arm select) on all native (#ifndef __EMSCRIPTEN__) builds. Severity kept at medium: it is a real lost-wakeup/deadlock with possible cross-list splicing, but manifestation is race-dependent (requires the specific concurrent-registration interleaving) rather than deterministic on every run; arguably high given a silent whole-program hang. || [high/medium] Verified against the actual source. include/channel.h:15-21 confirms LatSelectWaiter is an intrusive node with a SINGLE `next` pointer. channel.c:139-149 matches the quoted snippet exactly: channel_add_waiter does `w->next = ch->waiters; ch->waiters = w;` under that channel's own ch->mutex. All three backends (eval.c:10172/10234, stackvm.c:8144/8238, regvm.c:5998/6100) allocate ONE stack waiter per select and register that same `&waiter` on every channel arm. confirmed.title: process_shell() deadlocks (hang/DoS) when child writes large output to both stdout and stderr
confirmed.code:
char *stdout_buf = read_all_fd(stdout_pipe[0]);
    char *stderr_buf = read_all_fd(stderr_pipe[0]);
confirmed.trigger: From a Lattice program: shell("perl -e 'print STDOUT q{a}; print STDERR q{b} x 200000; print STDOUT q{c} x 200000'")  (any command that emits a small amount on stdout, then >64KB on stderr while still having stdout output pending). Reachable via the `shell` builtin in all backends (native_shell in runtime.c:2221 and eval.c:5284).
confirmed.consequence: The parent drains the two pipes strictly sequentially with blocking reads (read_all_fd reads stdout to EOF before touching stderr) and no poll/select or non-blocking fds. When the child fills the ~64KB stderr pipe buffer, the child blocks on write(stderr) and never closes/advances stdout; the parent is blocked in read_all_fd(stdout_pipe[0]) waiting for stdout that will never come. Both processes block forever (classic pipe deadlock), hanging the interpreter indefinitely — a denial-of-service reachable from ordinary, well-behaved child commands.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] Verified against the actual file. src/process_ops.c:245-246 reads stdout to EOF via read_all_fd() (lines 24-50, a plain blocking read loop) BEFORE ever touching stderr; the parent closed its own write ends at 242-243 but the child still holds STDOUT_FILENO open until it exits. There is no poll/select/O_NONBLOCK anywhere in the file (grep confirmed none). This is a textbook two-pipe deadlock: if the child fills the ~64KB stderr pipe buffer it blocks in write(stderr), never exits, never closes stdout; the parent stays blocked in read(stdout) waiting for an EOF that can only arrive when the child exits. Reachability is fully confirmed with no upstream guard: `shell` is registered unconditionally as a native (runtime.c:4413 and 4022) and is also handled directly by the tree-walker (eval.c:5272-5288); both paths call process_shell() with the user-controlled command string, which goes straight to `/bin/sh -c`. The only upstream checks are arity (ac==1) and VAL_STR type — neither constrains child output. No kill-switch/sandbox/permission/env gate exists around the shell builtin. The trigger is actually broader than the finder states: any command writing >~64KB to stderr deadlocks regardless of stdout. Severity medium is appropriate: it is a genuine hang/DoS and a real robustness bug that breaks legitimate commands emitting large stderr, but it is not a privilege escalation since shell() already grants arbitrary command execution to the calling program, so an adversarial program author has stronger primitives anyway; the real-world harm is hanging on well-behaved commands the author didn't expect to block. || [high/medium] Verified directly in src/process_ops.c. read_all_fd (lines 24-50) is a blocking read loop terminating only on EOF (n==0, line 36) or non-EINTR error; no O_NONBLOCK, no timeout. The POSIX process_shell (lines 245-246) drains the two pipes strictly sequentially: stdout_buf = read_all_fd(stdout_pipe[0]) fully drains stdout to EOF before stderr is touched at line 246. grep of the file for fcntl/O_NONBLOCK/poll/select/FD_SET returns zero hits — the pipes created at 205-217 stay blocking. The child (229-239) dup2s both pipe write ends and execs /bin/sh -c cmd; parent closes only write ends (242-243). Mechanism holds: parent blocks in read(stdout) awaiting EOF, which only arrives when the child closes stdout (at exit). If the child first writes >pipe-capacity (~64KB on Linux) to stderr with stdout still open, it blocks in write(stderr) since nobody drains stderr_pipe[0] until line 246, which the parent never reaches — textbook two-pipe sequential-drain deadlock that hangs the interpreter. Reachable from the Lattice `shell` builtin via eval.c:5284 and runtime.c:2224. The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, or control flow. Not memory corruption and requires large stderr output while stdout is pending, so medium severity is correct. || [high/medium] Confirmed against the actual source. In process_shell() POSIX path (src/process_ops.c:204-271), the parent closes the write ends (242-243) then drains the two pipes strictly sequentially: stdout_buf = read_all_fd(stdout_pipe[0]) at line 245, then stderr_buf = read_all_fd(stderr_pipe[0]) at line 246, then waitpid at 251. read_all_fd (lines 24-50) is a plain blocking read() loop that runs until EOF (n==0) — it reads stdout completely before the parent ever touches stderr.

I actively searched for a refutation and found none: grep for O_NONBLOCK, poll(, select(, pthread in process_ops.c returns nothing, so the read fds are blocking and there is no concurrent draining (no second thread, no fcntl non-blocking, no multiplexing). The fds inherit default blocking mode from pipe(). This is the textbook two-pipe deadlock: a child that writes a little to stdout (keeping stdout open, no EOF) then >pipe-capacity to stderr (Linux default 64KB; the cited 200000-byte stderr write far exceeds it) blocks in write(stderr) because the parent never drains stderr until stdout hits EOF — which never happens because the child is blocked. The parent is wedged in read_all_fd(stdout_pipe[0]) forever. waitpid(251) is never reached, so the interpreter hangs.

Reachability confirmed: process_shell is invoked by the shell builtin from both backends — eval.c:5284 (tree-walk) and runtime.c:2224 (VM native). The Windows branch (162-203) and the Emscripten stub (317) read stdout-then-stderr too / are no-ops respectively; the WASM stub just errors out, but the native POSIX path is the live, vulnerable one. The second process_shell at line 317 is only the browser stub, not a mitigation.

Severity medium is appropriate: it is a hang/DoS, not memory corruption, and the caller already has command-execution capability via shell; but it triggers on ordinary, well-behaved verbose commands (e.g. a tool emitting many stderr warnings alongside stdout), so it is a genuine robustness/availability defect, not merely self-inflicted. No clone, bound, guard, or non-blocking mechanism refutes it.

confirmed.title: add_local has no 256-slot cap; local slots emitted as 1-byte operands silently truncate for functions with >256 locals
confirmed.trigger: A single function/scope that declares more than 256 locals (e.g. a long flat sequence of `let`/`flux` bindings, or large array/struct destructuring patterns that each add_local). resolve_local returns the >255 index, which is cast to uint8_t in every OP_GET_LOCAL/OP_SET_LOCAL/OP_SET_LOCAL_POP/OP_INC_LOCAL/OP_INDEX_LOCAL emit (e.g. line 483, 2367). local_cap doubles past 256, so the compiler never errors; the upvalue path by contrast does guard at 256 (line 222).
confirmed.consequence: Slot index wraps modulo 256, so reads/writes hit the wrong (but in-bounds) local slot: locals 256..N alias slots 0..N-256, including the reserved function-self slot 0. Result is silent miscompilation -- variables read/clobber each other's values -- not memory-unsafe (the truncated slot is always < the real local count, hence in-bounds), but a correctness/logic corruption with no diagnostic.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] VERIFIED against src/stackcompiler.c and confirmed by execution.

Static code: add_local (lines 194-206) has NO 256-slot cap. compiler_init sets local_cap=256 (line 56); add_local doubles it indefinitely (lines 195-198: `local_cap *= 2; realloc(...)`) and never sets compile_error. By contrast the upvalue path DOES guard: add_upvalue line 222-224 errors "too many upvalues in one function" at >=256, and the constant path errors at >65535 (line 112). Locals have no equivalent guard. resolve_local (208-213) returns the true index up to local_count-1, and every local emit truncates it: line 483 `emit_bytes(OP_GET_LOCAL, (uint8_t)slot)`, line 2367 `OP_SET_LOCAL_POP, (uint8_t)slot`, plus OP_INDEX_LOCAL (795), OP_SET_INDEX_LOCAL (2262, 2443), OP_INC/DEC_LOCAL (2337/2341), OP_APPEND_STR_LOCAL (2355), OP_SET_LOCAL (2389), OP_SET_SLICE_LOCAL (2414). So slot index wraps modulo 256.

Reachability: bindings become locals only when scope_depth>0 (line 2316); top-level bindings are globals (OP_DEFINE_GLOBAL). But function bodies start at scope_depth=1 (line 64), and slot 0 is reserved (lines 77-82), so a function with >255 user `let`/`flux` bindings (or large destructure/match patterns that each call add_local) accumulates local_count >256. No parser/typecheck/validator upstream rejects this — there is no MAX_LOCAL check anywhere (grep for 256/255/UINT8/too many confirms only the cap/upvalue/constant guards exist).

Empirical confirmation: a generated function with 257 sequential `let v1=1 ... let v257=257` bindings then `return v257` compiled with NO diagnostic and returned 1 instead of 257 — v257's true slot 257 was emitted as (uint8_t)257==1, reading physical slot 1 (v1). ASAN (clat_asan) reported no error and exited 0, confirming the truncated slot stays in-bounds: this is silent logic miscompilation, not memory unsafety.

Severity: keeping low. The trigger requires an atypical single function/scope with >256 locals (rare in hand-written code, plausible only in generated/very large functions), and there is no memory-safety impact (ASAN clean) — purely a silent wrong-result correctness bug. The clox heritage normally guards this exact case ("Too many local variables in function"); the omission is real but low-likelihood. Relevant file: /Users/alexjokela/projects/lattice/src/stackcompiler.c. || [high/low] Verified against the actual source and reproduced empirically.

CODE FACTS (src/stackcompiler.c):
- add_local (lines 194-206) only grows local_cap by doubling (line 196) and never checks any 256 ceiling. resolve_local (208-213) returns an int index up to local_count-1 with no clamping.
- Every local emit truncates with (uint8_t): OP_GET_LOCAL at line 483, OP_SET_LOCAL_POP at 2367, OP_APPEND_STR_LOCAL at 2355, OP_SET_LOCAL at 2389, plus the param/destructure/for-loop paths.
- The contrast in the claim is correct: the upvalue path DOES guard ("too many upvalues in one function", line 222 `if (comp->upvalue_count >= 256)`), the constant path guards (line 105), but the local path has no equivalent. A grep for any local-count cap found none.

NUANCE / PARTIAL CORRECTION OF THE FINDER'S TRIGGER: A flat top-level sequence of `let`/`flux` does NOT trigger this, because at FUNC_SCRIPT scope_depth==0 those bindings compile to OP_DEFINE_GLOBAL, not locals (line 2316-2320: `if (current->scope_depth > 0) add_local(...); else DEFINE_GLOBAL`). The bug requires >256 simultaneously-live locals inside a single function/block (scope_depth>=1) — e.g. a function with ~256+ `let` bindings, or large destructuring patterns that each add_local. The finder's "single function/scope" phrasing is the accurate framing; the "top-level flat sequence" example is wrong.

EMPIRICAL CONFIRMATION: Built test = a no-arg `fn f()` with 300 locals `a1..a300` each set to 1000+i, returning `a257` (expected 1257). Slot layout: slot 0 reserved, a1->1 ... a257->257. (uint8_t)257 == 1, so OP_GET_LOCAL reads slot 1 (a1). Running ./clat_asan printed `1001` (== a1's value 1000+1), not 1257, with no compile error or diagnostic. This exactly matches the predicted modulo-256 aliasing.

The truncated index slot&0xFF is always < the real local_count (since the function pushes all >256 locals onto the value stack), so frame_base+truncated stays within the live frame region; asan reported no error. Severity low is appropriate: triggering needs a pathological >256-local function, impact is logic corruption rather than a crash/RCE, and there is no diagnostic to warn the author. || [high/low] I tried to refute this and could not; the finding is correct and empirically reproducible.

Not memory-unsafe (truncated slot is always < real local count). Severity confirmed low: real correctness corruption but the trigger (>256 locals in a single function/scope) is rare in hand-written code (top-level lets become globals, not locals), though reachable via generated/large destructuring code. confirmed.title: Unchecked arena page allocation result dereferenced in arena_alloc / bump_alloc

confirmed.title: Integer literal overflow silently saturates to LLONG_MAX (strtoll errno not checked)

confirmed.title: Unchecked realloc() in JSON string/array growth -> NULL-deref write on allocation failure
confirmed.trigger: Call json_parse() (exposed to Lattice as the builtin json_parse(s) and the json.parse module fn, src/eval.c:4035, src/runtime.c:1303) on a very large attacker-supplied JSON string or array under memory pressure. On a memory-constrained host or the 32-bit WASM build (where the linear-memory cap makes a large alloc deterministically fail), realloc() returns NULL, the old block is leaked, and the immediately following buf[len++]=c / elems[len++]=elem writes through NULL+offset.

Inconsistent with the surrounding code, which DOES NULL-check the sibling malloc()s at lines 60, 195, 223 and inside jp_error (line 33). Same gap repeats at lines 110, 116, 123 (\u escape paths) and 141 (string) and 253 (array).

confirmed.title: \u escape handling: surrogate pairs not decoded (lone-surrogate / CESU-8 output) and � truncates string

confirmed.title: Set.remove() leaks the removed element value (lat_map_remove does not free inline value)
confirmed.trigger: flux s = Set::new(); s.add("leakme"); s.remove("leakme");  // the deep-cloned element stored in the set is leaked; loopable for unbounded growth
confirmed.consequence: Same root cause as Map.remove: the set stores value_deep_clone() copies that own heap memory, but lat_map_remove() never frees the inline value and the dispatcher (stackvm.c:2266-2273) frees only the key arg. Each removal of a non-scalar element permanently leaks its heap allocation.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] The leak is real and reachable for non-scalar elements; only the finder's literal-string example is a poor choice.

Mechanism verified end-to-end:
- A Set's backing map stores LatValue inline: value_set_new (value.c:301) creates lat_map_new(sizeof(LatValue)); lat_map_set (hashmap.c:93-114) memcpy's the LatValue struct into the entry's inline _ibuf.
- builtin_set_add (builtin_methods.c:1448-1450) stores value_deep_clone(&args[0]). For non-scalar/non-interned values, value_clone_impl (value.c:454-475) allocates heap that the inline LatValue OWNS: arrays get out.as.array.elems = lat_alloc(...) (malloc when no arena/heap active), non-interned strings get lat_strdup→malloc.
- builtin_set_remove (builtin_methods.c:1455-1462) calls lat_map_remove and frees only the hash-key string. lat_map_remove (hashmap.c:145-162) frees e->key and sets MAP_TOMBSTONE but explicitly does NOT free the inline value (comment "value is inline, no free needed"). The dispatcher (stackvm.c:2266-2273) frees only args[0] (the argument), not the stored clone.
- The ownership asymmetry is proven by value_free's VAL_SET case (value.c:1570-1581), which iterates each MAP_OCCUPIED entry and value_free()s the inline value on whole-set teardown — so those inline values genuinely own heap that remove() never reclaims.

Reachability: no upstream guard restricts set elements to scalars — value_hash_key (value.c:1444-1488) handles structs and falls back to value_display for arrays/strings, so Set::new()/.add()/.remove() accept any value, dispatched identically in all three backends (stackvm.c:2270 default, regvm.c:1992, eval.c:7977).

Caveat on the stated trigger: s.add("leakme") does NOT leak, because OP_CONSTANT interns all string constants (stackvm.c:3360-3363 value_string_interned), and value_clone_impl shares the interned pointer (value.c:459-461) with no heap alloc and no free needed. But the finder's CONSEQUENCE correctly generalizes to "non-scalar element," and genuinely reachable cases leak per removal: s.add([1,2,3]); s.remove([1,2,3]) (array, always malloc-backed), struct/map/nested-set elements, and runtime-built strings >64 bytes (INTERN_THRESHOLD, stackvm.c:48) which bypass interning. A loop adding+removing such elements leaks unboundedly. Severity low is correct: a slow memory leak gated on a specific add/remove pattern with non-scalar elements, no corruption or safety impact. || [high/low] Mechanism re-derived from source and confirmed. builtin_set_add (src/builtin_methods.c:1448-1452) stores value_deep_clone(&args[0]); for strings value_clone_impl VAL_STR (src/value.c:462-464) does lat_strdup -> a malloc-backed copy with region_id=-1, so the stored LatValue OWNS heap memory. The LatMapEntry stores the LatValue inline in _ibuf (include/ds/hashmap.h:20-25), but its as.str_val pointer still references separately-malloc'd heap. value_free's VAL_SET branch (src/value.c:1570-1581) explicitly calls value_free on every OCCUPIED entry's stored LatValue, proving those values are owned and must be freed. lat_map_remove (src/ds/hashmap.c:145-162) frees only e->key with the literal comment '/* value is inline, no free needed */' and sets MAP_TOMBSTONE — it never value_free's the inline value's heap pointers. builtin_set_remove (1455-1462) contains no value_free of the stored element; the dispatcher (stackvm.c:2270-2271, mirrored in regvm.c:1992-1994) only does value_free(&args[0]), which frees the lookup argument string, a distinct allocation from the stored clone. Tombstoned entries are skipped by both value_free and lat_map_free, so the orphaned string is never reclaimed even at set teardown. Trigger s.add("leakme"); s.remove("leakme") in a loop leaks one allocation per iteration, unbounded; arrays/structs leak more. No guard restricts elements to scalars. Confirmed leak; low severity is correct (no crash/UAF, deterministic per-removal leak that accumulates). || [high/low] Confirmed real. builtin_set_remove (src/builtin_methods.c:1455-1462) calls lat_map_remove without first value_free-ing the stored element. The set stores value_deep_clone() copies (builtin_set_add, builtin_methods.c:1449) in a map whose value_size = sizeof(LatValue) (value.c:301), inline in the entry's _ibuf. For a non-interned element the clone owns heap memory: value_clone_impl does lat_strdup for strings (value.c:463) and lat_alloc for array elems (value.c:469). lat_map_remove (hashmap.c:145-162) frees only e->key and tombstones the slot; its "value is inline, no free needed" comment refers solely to the LatValue struct in _ibuf, NOT the heap it owns. That the stored values must be freed is proven by value_free for VAL_SET (value.c:1570-1581), which loops every occupied entry calling value_free(sv) before lat_map_free, precisely because lat_map_free (hashmap.c:38-50) frees keys only. The tree-walker does it right (eval.c:7975-7977: old = lat_map_get; value_free(old); lat_map_remove), and the dispatcher (stackvm.c:2271) only frees args[0] (the argument), a separate allocation from the stored copy. The regvm path (regvm.c:1992-1997) has the identical omission. So removing a non-interned heap-owning element permanently leaks its allocation; loopable for unbounded growth. One nuance that lowers but does not refute: the finding's exact literal trigger "leakme" is a 6-char string and gets interned (INTERN_THRESHOLD=64, stackvm.c:48); interned strings share intern-table memory that value_free never frees (region_id==REGION_INTERNED), so that specific literal does not actually leak. A genuine leak needs a non-interned element — a string >64 chars or other non-scalar value the set holds. Severity low is appropriate: a slow leak requiring repeated removals of non-interned heap elements, no crash or memory unsafety.

confirmed.title: pow(INT64_MIN, even exponent): llabs(INT64_MIN) UB bypasses overflow guard, then base_val*=base_val overflows
confirmed.trigger: Lattice program: `pow(0 - 9223372036854775807 - 1, 2)` (base=INT64_MIN, exp=2). The exponent is even so the first overflow check (line 117, which would route to float) is skipped; control reaches the squaring guard at line 126. `llabs(INT64_MIN)` is UB (negation of INT64_MIN) and on common impls returns INT64_MIN (negative), so `INT64_MAX / llabs(base_val)` == 0 and the `> 0` test is false, so the float fallback is NOT taken; `base_val *= base_val` then overflows. Reproduced under clat_asan: `src/math_ops.c:126:44/:74 negation of -9223372036854775808 ...` and `src/math_ops.c:129:26 signed integer overflow`.
confirmed.consequence: Undefined behavior (negation overflow + signed multiply overflow). On mainstream platforms it does not crash but yields a silently wrong result (pow(INT64_MIN,2) returns 0 instead of falling through to a float). A sufficiently aggressive optimizer could miscompile this path.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] VERIFIED REACHABLE AND REAL. The cited code matches src/math_ops.c:126-129 exactly: `if (base_val != 0 && llabs(base_val) > INT64_MAX / llabs(base_val)) { ... } base_val *= base_val;`. 

Reachability chain (no upstream guard): pow() is a registered 2-arg native reachable from any Lattice program via the VM (src/runtime.c:4251 rt_register_native, module export 3892) and the tree-walker (src/eval.c:4355-4362 calls math_pow directly). The MATH2 macro (src/runtime.c:676-687) and the eval.c call both forward args[0]/args[1] verbatim into math_pow with zero validation beyond arity. There is no type/range clamp, no rejection of INT64_MIN, no special-casing of base. I confirmed a trivial real program produces the base: `0 - 9223372036854775807 - 1` prints exactly -9223372036854775808 (INT64_MIN) under ./clat, and an even int exponent (2) is a plain literal.

Path trace for pow(INT64_MIN, 2): e=2 is not <0; result=1, base_val=INT64_MIN, exp_val=2. First loop iteration: exp_val&1==0 (even) so the line-117 odd-branch guard is skipped entirely; exp_val>>=1 ->1; control reaches line 126 with base_val==INT64_MIN. llabs(INT64_MIN) is negation of INT64_MIN = UB; INT64_MAX/llabs(...) yields 0 and the `>0` test is false, so the float fallback is NOT taken, and `base_val *= base_val` (line 129) overflows.

I reproduced the exact loop body standalone under clang -fsanitize=undefined: it emitted `negation of -9223372036854775808 cannot be represented` at the llabs calls (matching :126) and `signed integer overflow: INT64_MIN * INT64_MIN` (matching :129), with final result res=0. Meanwhile the optimized ./clat build returned the float 8.50706e+37 — exactly the build-dependent divergence the finding describes (a hallmark of UB; on -O0/asan you get the silently-wrong 0). 

This is genuine, triggerable undefined behavior reachable from one line of user code. Severity low is correct: numeric edge case (INT64_MIN base + even exponent), no memory corruption, worst observed consequence is a silently incorrect result (0 instead of ~8.5e37) on some toolchains, plus latent miscompilation risk. The same llabs(INT64_MIN) flaw also exists at line 117-118 for odd exponents (e.g. pow(INT64_MIN,1)), reinforcing that this is a real defect rather than a one-off. || [high/low] Verified against src/math_ops.c:93-139. Iter 1: exp_val&1==0 so the line-117 odd-bit overflow check is skipped (as claimed); exp_val>>=1 -> 1 (>0), reaching line 126. llabs(base_val)==llabs(INT64_MIN): argument is LLONG_MIN on a 64-bit platform where long long==int64_t, and llabs of the most-negative value is UB (line 126 has two such calls). On mainstream impls it returns INT64_MIN (negative), so INT64_MAX/llabs(base_val)==0 and the guard INT64_MIN>0 is false, so the float fallback at line 127 is NOT taken. Line 129 then executes base_val*=base_val = INT64_MIN*INT64_MIN = 2^126, a signed-overflow UB; wraparound yields 0. Iter 2 (exp_val=1): base_val is now 0, so line 117's base_val!=0 short-circuits, result*=0 -> 0. Function returns value_int(0) instead of routing to float pow (~8.5e37). Both UB sites (negation at :126, signed multiply at :129) and the silently-wrong 0 result are real. The finder did not misread control flow, bounds, or types. I could not find any clone/guard/early-return that refutes it; the finder's snippet matches the file verbatim. confirmed.title: Out-of-range / NaN double-to-int64 conversions are undefined behavior (to_int, floor, ceil, round)
confirmed.code: return value_int((int64_t)v->as.float_val);
confirmed.trigger: Lattice: `to_int(pow(10.0, 30.0))` (also `floor(big)`/`ceil(big)`/`round(big)` via src/math_ops.c:40,53,66, and `to_int(0.0/0.0)` for NaN). Casting a double whose magnitude exceeds INT64 range (or NaN/inf) directly to int64_t is UB. Reproduced under clat_asan: `src/type_ops.c:15:26 runtime error: 1e+30 is outside the range of representable values of type 'long long'`.
confirmed.consequence: Undefined behavior with platform-dependent garbage results: arm64 saturates to INT64_MAX, x86-64 yields INT64_MIN, NaN is implementation-defined. No crash on mainstream hardware, but values are silently wrong and the conversion is UB the compiler may assume never happens. No range/NaN guard exists before any of these casts.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] Verified the cited code directly. src/type_ops.c:15 inside type_to_int's VAL_FLOAT case is exactly `return value_int((int64_t)v->as.float_val);` with no range/NaN guard. math_floor/ceil/round at src/math_ops.c:40,53,66 are the same unguarded `(int64_t)floor/ceil/round(v->as.float_val)` casts.

No guard, clone, bound, or compiler invariant prevents the trigger; `to_int(pow(10.0,30.0))` is trivially expressible in real Lattice source.
